title: Update ARCHITECTURE.md to reflect MessageBus, Agent, History, and Initial Build decisions
description:
## Goal
Bring `ARCHITECTURE.md` into sync with the architectural decisions made during the post-Phase-1 conceptual-integrity work: §MessageBus surface, §Agent outbound vocabulary, §History framing, and the §Initial Build pseudocode at the end of the file. The §Harness paragraph (already updated by bd-nqo) and the §Model paragraph (already updated by bd-wnw) are STAYING AS-IS — do not retouch them.

This is a documentation-only Bead. No source code changes. The only file that may be modified is `ARCHITECTURE.md`.

## Reference
- `ARCHITECTURE.md` (the file you are editing)
- `src/messagebus.rs` — for the actual trait surface to describe (read; do not change)
- `src/agent.rs` — for the actual outbound event vocabulary (read; do not change)
- `src/lib.rs::run_async` — for the actual lifecycle pattern that the §Initial Build pseudocode should mirror (read; do not change)

## Required edits

### 1. §MessageBus

Replace the existing §MessageBus paragraph (which currently ends with "...not require any Agent or Harness changes." followed by a paragraph beginning with "While implementation of the MessageBus is deferred for now...") with the following text:

```markdown
The MessageBus is owned by the Harness. It is the routing fabric between Agents,
and between Agents and the Harness. Each Agent publishes its outbound events
through the MessageBus; subscribers (the Harness, peer Agents, external clients)
consume them through the same surface.

The MessageBus is defined as a trait. Its surface deliberately does NOT expose
concrete channel types (no `broadcast::Sender`, no `mpsc::Receiver`). Instead the
trait exposes:

- `register(agent_id)` — produces the single-consumer inbound stream for an Agent.
- `publish(agent_id, event)` — broadcast an outbound `AgentEvent` for an Agent.
- `subscribe(agent_id)` — obtain a fresh multi-consumer stream of an Agent's
  outbound `AgentEvent`s.
- `send_inbound(agent_id, block)` — push a `Block` onto an Agent's inbound stream.
- `route(from, to)` — forward AgentMessage Blocks from one Agent's outbound to
  another Agent's inbound; the bridge between independent Agents.

The trait returns abstract `Stream` types throughout. Phase 1 ships
`InMemoryMessageBus`, a single-process implementation backed by Tokio channels.
The trait surface is intentionally shaped so that future implementations spanning
process boundaries — for client isolation, distributed agents, or persistence —
can be added without changing the `Agent`, `Harness`, or any consumer code. The
Tokio-channel mechanics are an implementation detail of `InMemoryMessageBus`,
not a contract of the trait.
```

This replaces BOTH paragraphs (the "owned by the Harness ... routing fabric ..." paragraph AND the "While implementation of the MessageBus is deferred for now ..." paragraph). The "deferred for now" framing is wrong — the bus shipped — and should be removed.

### 2. §Agent

The §Agent section currently describes the outbound stream as carrying "two kinds of events: completed Blocks (those it has appended to History) and partial streaming tokens from the active Model call." That's stale. Replace the paragraph that begins "The Agent's outbound stream carries two kinds of events" with:

```markdown
The Agent's outbound stream carries `AgentEvent`s of four kinds:

- `TokenDelta { text }` — a partial chunk of the active Model call's streaming
  output. Best-effort delivery; slow consumers may miss intermediate deltas.
- `BlockComplete { block }` — a `HistoryNode` has been appended to the Agent's
  History. Subscribers can rebuild a faithful view of the Agent's History by
  subscribing before the run starts and accumulating these events.
- `RunComplete { usage }` — the current model call has finished. Carries the
  reported token usage.
- `Error { message }` — the Agent's task exited with an error. Emitted by the
  Harness on behalf of the failed Agent; other Agents continue running.

The stream is multi-subscriber; subscribers filter to the kinds they care about.
```

Then, the paragraph that currently begins "An Agent is constantly watching its stream for incoming messages, and serializes these messages. Whenever a message is accepted, it is immediately appended to the History log. Then, that History log is marshalled into a context which is sent out to the Model configured with the Agent." should be updated to reflect the MessageBus-mediated inbound stream and the `ModelInput` shape. Replace it with:

```markdown
An Agent's inbound stream is provided by the MessageBus (via `register`). The
Agent reads inbound `Block`s one at a time and serializes their processing:
each Block is appended to History; the History is then compiled into a
`ModelInput` and dispatched to the Agent's `Default` Model. Streaming model
output is published to the Agent's outbound stream as `AgentEvent`s, the final
assistant response is appended to History as an `AgentMessage` Block, and the
Agent awaits the next inbound Block. Agents are limited by design to one
pending Model call at a time; this invariant falls out of the single-task event
loop and requires no explicit synchronization.
```

Finally, the standalone sentence at the end of §Agent — "Agents are limited by design to having only one pending call to the Model at one time." — is now redundant with the paragraph above. REMOVE it. Also remove the trailing two-sentence paragraph beginning "In this way, the Agent is responsible for appending to that History as blocks get completed, both input blocks ... and output blocks ..." — that point is now covered by the rewritten paragraph.

### 3. §History

The current §History paragraph correctly describes the linked-list-of-Arc<Block>-nodes shape, but doesn't frame it as the project's *durable event log*. That framing came up in the post-review discussion and is worth committing to. ADD a short paragraph immediately AFTER the existing bullet list of Block variants:

```markdown
History is the Agent's durable event log: an append-only, immutable record of
every Block the Agent has observed or produced. It is the lossless ground
truth from which any model input must be derived. Higher-level constructs —
context compilation, summarization, selective retrieval, vector-store
augmentation — are operations OVER History, not replacements for it. The
Block enum lists the engine's primitive event kinds; domain-specific agent
implementations may layer their own event types alongside History without
extending Pristine's core Block vocabulary.
```

Do NOT change the existing first paragraph of §History or the bullet list of Block variants.

### 4. §Initial Build (pseudocode at the end of the file)

The pseudocode currently shows:
```
let fut = harness.start();
...
harness.shutdown()
await fut
```

This reflects the OLD lifecycle that bd-nqo redesigned. Update the pseudocode body to mirror what `src/lib.rs::run_async` actually does today. The replacement (preserving the "Code is indicative only" framing — this is still pseudocode, not Rust):

```
fn main() {
    let mut harness = HarnessBuilder::new()
        .add_model(ModelId("anthropic-default"), AnthropicModel::build(...))
        .add_agent(PendingAgent { id, system_prompt: "You are the Pristine agent. ...", model_id })
        .build()?;

    harness.start()?;

    let mut events = harness.subscribe(agent_id)?;

    harness.send_to_agent(agent_id, owner_id, "Introduce yourself to me, Pristine");

    // Stream tokens to stdout until the first RunComplete arrives.

    harness.send_to_agent(agent_id, owner_id, "Write me a poem of what it is like to be you, Pristine");

    // Stream tokens to stdout until the second RunComplete arrives.

    harness.shutdown();
    harness.join().await?;
}
```

Keep the surrounding "Code is indicative only, not proper syntax or naming" framing. Do NOT introduce Rust async-fn or imports — the section is intentionally pseudo-rust to convey intent. The point is that the pseudocode reflects the SHAPE of the actual binary loop, which is now `start` → `subscribe` → `send` → `recv` → `send` → `recv` → `shutdown` → `join.await`.

### 5. Optional but encouraged: §Goals

The §Goals list mentions "Support for multiple parallel agents running within one process" and "Support for peer message sending between Agents". Both are now structurally supported by the per-agent fault-isolation work and the `MessageBus::route` method. No edit required, but if you naturally end up rewording these for crispness while making other edits, that's fine. Do NOT add new bullet points; the list is intentionally short.

### 6. Do NOT edit

- §Harness lifecycle paragraph (bd-nqo authoritative)
- §Model paragraphs about `ModelInput` and `ContextCompiler` (bd-wnw authoritative)
- §Examples (still accurate)
- §Non-goals (still accurate)
- §Key Technological Choices (still accurate)
- The opening §Goals bullet list (unless you have a clear-improvement edit per §5 above)

## Style
- Match the existing prose register: short paragraphs, no banners, no nested headings beyond `###`.
- No code fences inside the architecture sections except where the original file already used them (the §Initial Build pseudocode block).
- Backticks for type names (`ModelInput`, `AgentEvent`, etc.).

## Files you MAY modify
- `ARCHITECTURE.md` (this is the ONLY file you may touch)

## Files you may NOT touch
- Any file under `src/`
- Any file under `tests/`
- `Cargo.toml`, `Cargo.lock`
- `STYLE_GUIDE.md`, `README.md`, `AGENTS.md`, `TESTING.md`, `blueprint/`
- `.beads/`

## Verification
- `cargo fmt --check`, `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings`, `cargo nextest run` — all unchanged (doc-only edit; the build remains green at 31 passed + 1 skipped).
- `grep -n 'two kinds of events' ARCHITECTURE.md` returns ZERO matches.
- `grep -n 'deferred for now' ARCHITECTURE.md` returns ZERO matches.
- `grep -n 'await fut' ARCHITECTURE.md` returns ZERO matches.
- `grep -n 'AgentEvent\|MessageBus.*publish\|durable event log\|join().await' ARCHITECTURE.md` returns several matches (the new content is present).

## Report
- The four section updates, line numbers in the new file.
- `jj diff --summary` confirming only `ARCHITECTURE.md` (and Coordinator-owned `.beads/*`) changed.
- Confirm `cargo nextest run` still shows 31 passed + 1 skipped.

## Out of scope
- Any code change. This is documentation only.
- §Harness, §Model, §Examples, §Non-goals, §Key Technological Choices — leave untouched.
- New sections or new bullets beyond what the bead specifies.

title: Add jsonrpsee dependency and serde derives on AgentId/UserId
description: Add jsonrpsee = { version = "0.26.0", features = ["server-core", "macros"] } to Cargo.toml. Add Serialize/Deserialize derives to AgentId and UserId in src/history.rs (needed for JSON-RPC response serialization). Verify cargo check passes.

title: Phase B1: src/config module skeleton + typed structs with serde derives
description:
Plan: blueprint/configuration-file/plan-configuration-file.md (Phase B1).

Create src/config/ as a non-mod-rs module (i.e. src/config.rs declaring submodules in src/config/*.rs). Define typed structs:
- TopologyConfig: shape that mirrors the embedded default.toml — agents (with name, system_prompt, model alias, tools list), tools (named tool registrations), prompts. Keep it close to the eventual default.toml structure described in the plan.
- AuthConfig: providers (provider name + provider-specific fields like base_url), models (alias -> { provider, model_name, credentials }).
- Config: the inert resolved value returned to main (post-templating, post-resolution).
- ConfigError: typed enum of every distinct config failure (TomlParse with line/col, UnknownEnvVar, DanglingAlias, UndeclaredTool, UnknownProvider, IoError, etc.).
- ConfigErrors: Vec-backed aggregate of ConfigError.

Constraints:
- All structs derive Deserialize + Debug + Clone (where it makes sense).
- All structs that come from TOML have #[serde(deny_unknown_fields)].
- No parsing or resolution logic yet — types only. This bead is purely declarative.
- New dependency: add 'toml' crate to Cargo.toml. Use the latest stable.
- Unit test that confirms the structs compile and that deny_unknown_fields rejects a struct literal with an extra field (use toml::from_str in a test).

Files to read: blueprint/configuration-file/plan-configuration-file.md (whole plan), src/tool.rs (ToolError pattern), src/lib.rs (current hardcoded shape — informs default.toml layout).

Definition of Done: cargo fmt --check, cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features, cargo nextest run all pass.

title: Resolve duplicated timestamp state between HistoryNode and Block variants
description:
There are two parallel sources of truth for "when did this block happen":

1. `HistoryNode.timestamp: SystemTime` set by `History::append` to `SystemTime::now()` at insertion time (src/history.rs:102, 151).
2. `Block::UserMessage { timestamp }`, `Block::ReasoningTrace { timestamp }`, `Block::ToolCall { timestamp }`, `Block::ToolResult { timestamp }`, `Block::AgentMessage { timestamp }` — each variant carries its own `timestamp` field (src/history.rs:76, 80, 85, 90, 95).

`History::append` never reads the Block-internal timestamp; the test helper `user_message` sets its own `SystemTime::now()` inside the Block independently. This will quietly drift: once Step 4 (Anthropic ARModel) starts producing Blocks, the wall-clock value inside a Block and the one on its HistoryNode will diverge in ways no caller has spec'd.

Required: pick ONE source of truth.

Recommended resolution (smallest diff): remove the `timestamp` field from each `Block` variant. `HistoryNode` already carries the canonical insertion timestamp and is the authoritative wrapper. Callers that need a timestamp use `HistoryNode::timestamp()`.

Alternate resolution: remove `HistoryNode.timestamp` and require every Block variant to carry its own. Less preferable because (a) the wrapping node is the natural place and (b) Block variants would still each duplicate the field.

Acceptance:
- Exactly one timestamp field remains per logical event.
- All call-sites and the 3 history tests compile and pass.
- cargo fmt --check, cargo clippy -D warnings, cargo nextest run all clean.

Resolve before Step 4 lands so the Anthropic ARModel implementation doesn't bake in the duplication.

title: Phase F3: Integration test in tests/ loading default.toml + fixture auth
description:
Plan: blueprint/configuration-file/plan-configuration-file.md (Phase F3).

Add an integration test under tests/ that loads the embedded default.toml plus a fixture pristine-auth.toml and asserts the resulting Harness has the expected agent, model, and tool registrations. No live API calls.

Constraints:
- The fixture pristine-auth.toml lives under tests/fixtures/ (create the directory if needed).
- The test sets ANTHROPIC_API_KEY (or the relevant env var) to a stub value via std::env::set_var() so templating succeeds without contacting Anthropic.
- The test constructs LoadArgs pointing config=None (so embedded default.toml is used) and auth=Some(fixture_path), runs pristine_config::load(), then walks the Config into a HarnessBuilder, and asserts:
  * one agent with the expected name
  * the agent's model resolves to AnthropicProvider with the expected model_name
  * the agent's tool list contains the five built-in tools (read, write, edit, insert, exec_bash)
- The test does NOT call Harness::start() / start any tasks. It only verifies the construction surface.

Files to read: blueprint/configuration-file/plan-configuration-file.md, src/config/, src/lib.rs, default.toml.

Definition of Done: cargo fmt --check, cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features, cargo nextest run all pass.

title: Insert: types + impl + tests + registration (full slice)
description:
## Context

Compressed Insert bead. Final per-tool bead in the inter-phase. Uses shared `resolve_path` and `atomic_write` from `src/builtins/path.rs`.

## Required reading

1. PLAN.md (sections "Insert" under behavioral spec; "Per-tool error enums" -> InsertError)
2. PROMPT.md (the "Insert" bullet)
3. src/builtins/read.rs / write.rs / edit.rs (templates)
4. src/builtins/path.rs (shared helpers)

## Required deliverables

### 1. NEW `src/builtins/insert.rs`

```rust
use crate::builtins::path::{atomic_write, resolve_path, AtomicWriteError, PathResolveError};
use crate::tool::ToolError;

#[derive(serde::Deserialize)]
struct InsertInput {
    path: String,
    after_line: usize,
    content: String,
}

#[derive(serde::Serialize)]
#[serde(tag = "kind", rename_all = "snake_case")]
enum InsertError {
    FileNotFound { path: String },
    NotUtf8 { byte_offset: usize },
    InvalidAfterLine { after_line: usize, total_lines: usize },
    InvalidPath { reason: String },
    IoError { reason: String },
}

#[derive(serde::Serialize)]
struct InsertOutput {
    lines_inserted: usize,
}

pub struct Insert;

impl Insert {
    pub fn new() -> Self { Insert }
}

impl Default for Insert { fn default() -> Self { Self::new() } }

// Tool impl using #[jsonrpsee::core::async_trait].
```

### 2. Tool::call behavior

1. Resolve path → `InvalidPath` on PathResolveError.
2. Read file: `tokio::fs::read`. NotFound → `FileNotFound { path }`. Other IO → `IoError`.
3. UTF-8 validate: → `NotUtf8 { byte_offset: e.valid_up_to() }` on Err.
4. Compute `total_lines` as the number of `\n` chars in the content, plus 1 if the content is non-empty AND does NOT end with `\n`. This is the "logical line count" that matches user intuition.
   - Empty file: total_lines = 0.
   - "a": total_lines = 1.
   - "a\n": total_lines = 1.
   - "a\nb": total_lines = 2.
   - "a\nb\n": total_lines = 2.
5. If `after_line > total_lines`: `InvalidAfterLine { after_line, total_lines }`.
6. **Empty content special case**: if `content.is_empty()`, treat as no-op (do not write, return `lines_inserted: 0` to avoid trivial atomic writes). Document with `// NOTE:`.
7. Otherwise compute the new content:
   - Split the original by `\n` keeping the structure. Use this strategy:
     ```rust
     // Split content into lines (preserving structure carefully).
     let lines: Vec<&str> = if original.is_empty() {
         Vec::new()
     } else if original.ends_with('\n') {
         original[..original.len()-1].split('\n').collect()
     } else {
         original.split('\n').collect()
     };
     ```
     Now `lines.len() == total_lines`.
   - Compute the insertion content. Strip a trailing newline from `parsed.content` if present (so the inserted body is just the lines). Then split it by `\n`.
   - Insert the new lines at position `after_line` in the `lines` vec.
   - Join the result with `\n`. If the original ended with `\n` OR (after_line == total_lines AND there were original lines), the new content should also end with `\n`.

   Simpler alternative: construct the new content as a String by:
   - Iterating original by lines (including indices).
   - Emitting line N. After emitting line `after_line`, emit the insertion content with a trailing `\n` if not already.
   - Continue with remaining lines.
   - Preserve the trailing newline of the original (if it had one).

   Choose whichever is clearer to implement; the test suite below pins down behavior.

8. `lines_inserted` = number of `\n` in the inserted content, plus 1 if the inserted content is non-empty AND doesn't end with `\n` (same formula as total_lines above, applied to `parsed.content`).
9. Atomic-write via `atomic_write`. Map `AtomicWriteError` to `IoError`.
10. Return `InsertOutput { lines_inserted }`.

### 3. Edge cases

- `after_line == 0`: prepend.
- `after_line == total_lines`: append.
- `after_line > total_lines`: typed error.
- Empty `content`: no-op, `lines_inserted: 0`.
- Inserted content without trailing newline: append `\n` so the file stays well-formed when inserting mid-file. Documented behavior.
- Original file without trailing newline, appending: add `\n` between original last line and inserted content.

### 4. Update `src/builtins.rs`

Add `pub mod insert;` and `pub use insert::Insert;`.

### 5. Register in `src/lib.rs`

Add `.add_tool(Arc::new(Insert::new()))`. Widen import:
```rust
use crate::builtins::{AddTool, Edit, ExecBash, Insert, Read, Write};
```

### 6. ARCHITECTURE.md

Replace `### Insert` stub:

```markdown
### Insert

Inserts text at a specified line position in a UTF-8 text file. Input:
`{path, after_line: usize, content}`. Output: `{lines_inserted: usize}`.
The path is resolved (absolute or cwd-relative); the file is read in
full and UTF-8 validated. `after_line` is 1-indexed in the
intuitive sense: `after_line == 0` prepends, `after_line == total_lines`
appends, and any value in between inserts between line `after_line` and
line `after_line + 1`. `after_line` past the end of the file returns
`InvalidAfterLine { after_line, total_lines }`. Empty `content` is a
no-op success returning `lines_inserted: 0`. Content without a trailing
newline gets one appended to keep the file well-formed when inserting
mid-file; if the original file lacked a trailing newline, one is added
before the inserted content during append. The file write goes through
the shared atomic-rename helper. Errors: `FileNotFound { path }`,
`NotUtf8 { byte_offset }`, `InvalidAfterLine { after_line, total_lines }`,
`InvalidPath { reason }`, `IoError { reason }`.
```

### 7. Unit tests

At least 9 `#[tokio::test]`s:

1. `insert_in_middle_of_file` — file `"a\nb\nc\n"`, `after_line: 1, content: "X\n"`. Expected: `"a\nX\nb\nc\n"`. `lines_inserted == 1`.
2. `insert_prepends_when_after_line_zero` — file `"a\nb\n"`, `after_line: 0, content: "X"`. Expected: `"X\na\nb\n"`. `lines_inserted == 1`.
3. `insert_appends_when_after_line_equals_total` — file `"a\nb\n"`, `after_line: 2, content: "X"`. Expected: `"a\nb\nX\n"`. `lines_inserted == 1`.
4. `insert_returns_invalid_after_line_when_past_end` — file `"a\nb\n"`, `after_line: 99`. Expected `kind == "invalid_after_line"`, `after_line == 99`, `total_lines == 2`.
5. `insert_empty_content_is_noop_success` — file `"a\n"`, `after_line: 1, content: ""`. Expected `Ok({lines_inserted: 0})`. File unchanged.
6. `insert_multiline_content` — file `"a\nb\n"`, `after_line: 1, content: "X\nY"`. Expected `"a\nX\nY\nb\n"`. `lines_inserted == 2`.
7. `insert_into_empty_file` — file `""`, `after_line: 0, content: "X"`. Expected `"X\n"`. `lines_inserted == 1`.
8. `insert_append_to_file_without_trailing_newline` — file `"a"` (no trailing \n), `after_line: 1, content: "X"`. Expected `"a\nX\n"`. `lines_inserted == 1`.
9. `insert_not_utf8` — fixture with invalid bytes, expect `kind == "not_utf8"`.
10. `insert_file_not_found` — nonexistent path, expect `kind == "file_not_found"`.
11. `insert_invalid_path_empty_string` — `path: ""`, expect `kind == "invalid_path"`.

(11 tests; aim for 9+.)

## Constraints

- No `unwrap()`/`expect()` in production.
- No new deps.
- Test count: 131 → ≥140 passed, 6 skipped.
- Files: `A src/builtins/insert.rs`, `M src/builtins.rs`, `M src/lib.rs`, `M ARCHITECTURE.md`.

## jj workflow

`jj desc -m "$(printf 'Close bd-XXX: Insert tool (types + impl + tests + registration)\n\nCo-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>\n')"`

## Definition of Done

- fmt / clippy / nextest clean.
- 140+ passed, 6 skipped.
- Trailer present.

## Plan-respect note

Insert reuses `resolve_path` and `atomic_write` (the portable file-policy layer) and owns its own typed errors + line-counting semantics (the Insert dialect).

title: Remove design-narration doc-comments in src/model.rs
description:
src/model.rs contains module- and item-level doc-comments that narrate design rationale rather than provide information useful to future readers, violating STYLE_GUIDE.md §Comments ("Documentation for modules MUST not contain usage examples" and "Comments grow too verbose when coding agents describe their current goal or task").

Specific offenders:
1. Line 2 (module doc): `//! `Usage` is a tuple variant on `ModelStreamEvent` so it shares the struct shape with `MessageComplete { usage: Usage }` without duplication.` — narrates a design tradeoff that is obvious from the type definitions five lines below.
2. Line 52 (ARModel doc): `/// `system_prompt` is passed explicitly because the Agent owns it (see plan.md §"Agent configuration").` — narrates rationale and cross-references blueprint/plan.md, which is user-curated and may move.

Required changes:
- Reduce src/model.rs line 1-2 to just `//! Model traits and provider submodules.`
- Reduce src/model.rs line 52 to either remove the doc-comment on `ARModel` entirely or replace with a one-line statement about what `complete` does (not why its parameters are shaped the way they are).

Acceptance: cargo fmt --check, cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings, and cargo nextest run all pass. No other code changes.

title: Built-in tools P-2: AddTool error refactor (introduce AddError)
description:
## Context

Prelude bead 2/4. Now that `ToolError::Execution(serde_json::Value)` is in place (bd-3ll), `AddTool` is the first tool to adopt the per-tool typed-error pattern. This bead is also a template: every subsequent built-in tool will follow exactly this AddError pattern (typed enum + per-tool err helper).

## Required reading before coding

1. blueprint/builtin-tools/PLAN.md (sections "Per-tool error enums", "P-2 AddTool error refactor")
2. blueprint/builtin-tools/PROMPT.md (the "Error model" and "AddTool" bullets)
3. src/builtins.rs (current AddTool::call and its tests; verify it now returns `ToolError::Execution(serde_json::Value)` from bd-3ll)
4. ARCHITECTURE.md (the "Portable shape, adapter dialect" principle)

## Goal

Introduce a typed `AddError` enum private to `src/builtins.rs` (the file is NOT split into submodules yet -- that lands later). Refactor `AddTool::call` to construct typed errors and serialize them to JSON through the portable `ToolError::Execution(Value)` carrier.

## Required changes

1. In `src/builtins.rs`, add a private typed error enum:
   ```rust
   #[derive(serde::Serialize)]
   #[serde(tag = "kind", rename_all = "snake_case")]
   enum AddError {
       InvalidInput { reason: String },
   }
   ```

2. Add a per-tool helper (private to this module):
   ```rust
   fn err(e: AddError) -> ToolError {
       let value = serde_json::to_value(e)
           .unwrap_or_else(|_| serde_json::json!({"kind": "internal_error"}));
       ToolError::Execution(value)
   }
   ```
   (Use `unwrap_or_else`, not `unwrap`/`expect`, per RUST_STYLE_GUIDE.md.)

3. Refactor every `AddTool::call` error site to use `err(AddError::InvalidInput { reason: "...".into() })` instead of an ad-hoc string-wrapped JSON. The previous P-1 cascade left those as `ToolError::Execution(json!({"error": "..."}))`; this bead replaces them with the typed shape.

4. Update existing AddTool unit tests (look for `add_tool_rejects_*` and similar) so that on the error path they assert the JSON shape is `{"kind": "invalid_input", "reason": "..."}` -- not the older `{"error": "..."}` shape. Tests should `serde_json::from_value::<serde_json::Value>` the Execution payload and inspect the `kind` field.

## Out of scope

- Do NOT split src/builtins.rs into submodules. The submodule split happens organically with the first per-tool slice; AddTool will move when ARCHITECTURE doc-prep + tool-1 land.
- Do NOT add unused future variants to AddError. Only InvalidInput is needed right now.

## Definition of Done

- `cargo fmt --check` passes
- `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings` passes
- `cargo nextest run` passes within 120s
- One atomic commit with the project's standard trailer
- The diff is purely additive + replacement; no drive-by refactors

## Plan-respect note

This bead concretizes "Portable shape, adapter dialect": the typed enum is the dialect (per-tool), the serde_json::Value flowing through ToolError::Execution is the portable shape.

title: Reflection: bake commit-trailer template into coding agent prompt
description:
## Problem

Two of nine coding beads in the Built-in Tools inter-phase forgot the required `Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>` trailer on the close commit (bd-1cs retry, bd-3ql). The Coordinator had to repair commit metadata in-band, costing tokens and breaking the atomic-commit invariant.

The bd-2dr per-prompt fix (an exact `printf`-based `jj desc` template in the Coding Subagent's task message) worked. But it lives only in the Coordinator's per-bead prompt construction, so any new Coordinator instance can rediscover the failure mode.

3. Provides a `jj desc`-friendly recipe (HEREDOC or printf) the agent can copy verbatim.

Also edit `.claude/agents/judge.md` to add a "## Commit hygiene" check that explicitly tells the Judge to verify the trailer exists on the last commit before rendering PASS.

title: Fix stale tool-skipping claims in DESIGN.md and ARCHITECTURE.md
description:
## Problem

Three places in the project's narrative documentation still claim the Agent skips `ToolCall` and `ToolResult` blocks when compiling `ModelInput`. That was true through bd-2i9 but became false in bd-2t6, which replaced the skip with real emission as `ContentPart::ToolUse` / `ContentPart::ToolResult`. The claims now actively mislead any reader who lands on these docs before bd-1um is announced as complete.

### Specific stale claims

1. **ARCHITECTURE.md:72-73** — currently reads:
   > In Phase 1 the Agent constructs `ModelInput` by linearizing its History and skipping variants the model cannot consume (e.g. `ReasoningTrace`, `ToolCall`, `ToolResult`).

   Today the Agent skips ONLY `ReasoningTrace`. `ToolCall` and `ToolResult` are compiled into `ContentPart::ToolUse` and `ContentPart::ToolResult` (see `src/agent.rs` lines 193-218).

2. **DESIGN.md:21** (roadmap §1 "Tool Calls") — currently reads:
   > Currently the Agent skips `ToolCall` and `ToolResult` blocks when compiling History into `ModelInput` (see ARCHITECTURE.md History, Agent).

   Same staleness. Additionally, the §1 framing as an entirely future roadmap item is no longer accurate: the tool registry (bd-2i9), `ToolSpec` / tools array (bd-3e0, bd-29r), streaming `ToolUseStart`/`Delta`/`Complete` (bd-ex6, bd-2ur), History block compilation (bd-2t6), and dispatch loop (bd-1um) have all landed. What remains is demo-tool wiring (bd-8 forthcoming), the client.py REPL surface (bd-9 forthcoming), and these doc updates.

3. **DESIGN.md:13-15** ("Completed" section) — does not yet list "Tool Calls" as completed/in-progress. After bd-8 lands, the §1 roadmap entry should move into Completed.

## Required edits

Doc-only. No source code changes.

### ARCHITECTURE.md

Rewrite the paragraph at lines 72-77 to drop the false skipping claim:

```markdown
The Agent constructs `ModelInput` by linearizing its History and translating each
`Block` variant into the appropriate `Turn` / `ContentPart` shape: `UserMessage` and
`AgentMessage` become `Text` content parts on `User` / `Assistant` turns, `ToolCall`
becomes `ContentPart::ToolUse` on an `Assistant` turn, and `ToolResult` becomes
`ContentPart::ToolResult` on a `User` turn. `ReasoningTrace` is retained in History
but not routed to the model; ARMs vary on whether they accept it. In future phases
this construction will be extracted into a `ContextCompiler` trait so that selective
summarization, vector-store lookups, and sequence-to-sequence "context compilation"
can be plugged in without changing the Agent or the Model trait. The `ModelInput`
shape is the stable contract between compiler and model.
```

Drop the "In Phase 1 …" framing — that nomenclature was useful at the boundary of phases and is now ambient.

### DESIGN.md

Rewrite §1 "Tool Calls" in the Roadmap to either:

(a) Move it into the **Completed** section once bd-8 (demo-tool wiring) and bd-9 (client.py REPL surface) land, with a single bullet describing the realised scope.

(b) Until then, replace the "Currently the Agent skips …" sentence with:
   > The tool registry, `ToolSpec`/tools array, streaming tool-use events, History
   > block compilation, and dispatch loop have all landed; remaining work is wiring a
   > demo tool into `pristine run` and surfacing tool-call activity in `client.py`.

This bead implements option (b); option (a) is scheduled for the bead that closes out the demo + client work.

## Acceptance

- `grep -n 'skipping variants\|skips ToolCall\|skip `ToolCall`\|skips \`ToolCall\`' ARCHITECTURE.md DESIGN.md` returns ZERO matches.
- `grep -n 'ContentPart::ToolUse\|ContentPart::ToolResult' ARCHITECTURE.md` returns at least one match (the new content is present).
- `cargo fmt --check`, `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings`, `cargo nextest run` all still pass unchanged (doc-only).

## Out of scope

- §Harness, §MessageBus, §Client Protocol — unchanged.
- §Phase 1 / §Phase 2 narrative sections at the bottom of ARCHITECTURE.md — leave untouched. They are historical-record sections.
- No source code changes.

title: Phase 2 Step 8: Add builtins module with AddTool and wire it into pristine run
description:
Provide a small built-in tool so that `pristine run` can demonstrate the tool-call loop end-to-end against the real Anthropic API.

Tool of choice: `AddTool` -- takes `{"a": number, "b": number}`, returns `{"sum": number}`. Pedagogical, demonstrates structured input/output, exercises the dispatch loop without new dependencies.

Scope:

1. New module: src/builtins.rs (with `pub mod builtins;` line added to src/lib.rs).
   - Module doc-comment: single line `//! Built-in tools provided by the Pristine engine.`
   - Define `pub struct AddTool { schema: serde_json::Value }`.
   - `pub fn new() -> Self` constructor builds a JSON schema matching:
     ```json
     {
       "type": "object",
       "properties": {
         "a": {"type": "number"},
         "b": {"type": "number"}
       },
       "required": ["a", "b"]
     }
     ```
   - Implement `Default` returning `Self::new()`.
   - Implement `crate::tool::Tool`:
     - `name()` returns `"add"`.
     - `description()` returns `"Add two numbers and return their sum."`.
     - `input_schema()` returns `&self.schema`.
     - `async fn call(&self, input: serde_json::Value) -> Result<serde_json::Value, ToolError>`:
       - Deserialize `input` as a small typed struct `{ a: f64, b: f64 }` via `serde_json::from_value`. On parse failure return `ToolError::InvalidInput("AddTool requires numeric fields 'a' and 'b'".to_string())` (or include the underlying error text -- caller's discretion, just be precise).
       - Return `Ok(serde_json::json!({"sum": a + b}))`.

2. Tests inside src/builtins.rs `mod tests`:
   - `add_tool_returns_sum_for_valid_input` -- construct AddTool; call with `{"a":2,"b":3}`; assert result is `{"sum":5}`.
   - `add_tool_handles_negative_and_fractional` -- call with `{"a":-1.5,"b":4.25}`; assert `{"sum":2.75}`.
   - `add_tool_rejects_missing_fields` -- call with `{"a":1}` (missing b); assert `Err(ToolError::InvalidInput(_))`.
   - `add_tool_rejects_non_numeric_fields` -- call with `{"a":"hello","b":1}`; assert `Err(ToolError::InvalidInput(_))`.
   - `add_tool_schema_advertises_required_fields` -- read `tool.input_schema()`; assert `schema["required"]` contains both "a" and "b".

3. Wire AddTool into `src/lib.rs`'s `run_async`:
   - Add `use std::sync::Arc;` (already present -- verify).
   - Add `use crate::builtins::AddTool;` near other imports.
   - In `run_async`, after constructing the HarnessBuilder with the model and agent but BEFORE `.build()`, insert:
     ```rust
     .add_tool(Arc::new(AddTool::new()))
     .map_err(|e| anyhow::anyhow!("failed to register AddTool: {e}"))?
     ```
     -- chain syntax already used elsewhere in lib.rs; preserve the chain. The `.map_err` converts `harness::Error::DuplicateTool(_)` into an `anyhow::Error` so the function's return type stays compatible.
   - Confirm the binary still compiles and the typical happy path is unaffected.

4. Update the system prompt in src/lib.rs (the SYSTEM_PROMPT constant) to mention tool availability:
   - Append a sentence to the existing SYSTEM_PROMPT: `"You have access to an `add` tool that returns the sum of two numbers; use it when arithmetic is requested."`
   - This is a single line, minimal addition. Do NOT refactor SYSTEM_PROMPT into a constants module or split the string -- keep it inline.

Out of scope -- DO NOT DO:
- Touch tests/anthropic_live.rs (live-API tool-call smoke test will be added in a follow-up bead, not this one).
- Update DESIGN.md or ARCHITECTURE.md (bd-10 does that).
- Update client.py (bd-9 does that).
- Add new tools beyond AddTool (e.g. SubTool, EchoTool).
- Add dependencies.
- Modify src/agent.rs, src/harness.rs, src/tool.rs, src/messagebus.rs, src/history.rs, src/model.rs, src/model/anthropic.rs, src/stdio.rs, src/rpc.rs (the previous bead bd-2i9 already plumbed the registry through).
- Refactor src/lib.rs into smaller modules.

Constraints:
- No new dependencies.
- No `unwrap`/`expect` outside `#[cfg(test)]`.
- RUST_STYLE_GUIDE.md: no banner comments, no decorative dividers, no usage examples in doc-comments.
- `AddTool::call` must deserialize via `serde_json::from_value` into a small typed struct -- do NOT hand-roll JSON walking.
- All five tests must pass and be deterministic (no time-sensitivity, no random seeds).

Definition of Done:
- `cargo fmt --check`
- `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features` (zero warnings)
- `cargo nextest run` (within 120s, test count = 85: 80 prior + 5 new)
- A manual run of `pristine run` works without error if ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is set (out of scope for the agent to verify, but `cargo build` must succeed).

Commit policy: one atomic commit. Do NOT modify .beads/issues.jsonl or .beads/last-touched. Use the trailer `Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>`.

title: EOC-3: chained live integration test (Read -> Edit -> ExecBash)
description:
## Context

The chained live integration test for the Built-in Tools inter-phase. `#[ignore]`-gated, `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`-guarded. Exercises the entire stack: Harness construction with all five tools registered, a real Anthropic model call, multi-turn tool-call dispatch (Read → Edit → ExecBash), and assertion that the agent successfully ran the modified Python file.

## Required reading

1. blueprint/builtin-tools/PLAN.md (the "End-of-cycle integration test" section)
2. blueprint/builtin-tools/PROMPT.md (the end-of-cycle bullet)
3. tests/exec_bash_smoke.rs (existing `#[ignore]`-gated test using `ExecBash::new()`)
4. Look for existing live-model tests:
   - `tests/anthropic_live.rs` (if it exists — search via `rg "ANTHROPIC_API_KEY" tests/`)
   - Other `#[ignore]`-gated tests in `tests/` for harness construction patterns
5. `src/lib.rs::run_async` (or wherever `HarnessBuilder` is constructed) — study the registration chain and the model wiring
6. `src/harness.rs` (or equivalent) — the Harness API for sending UserMessages and subscribing to AgentEvent streams
7. `src/agent.rs` — the AgentEvent variants (look for `Idle`, `BlockComplete`, `AgentMessage`)
8. RUST_STYLE_GUIDE.md / TESTING.md

## Goal

Add `tests/builtin_tools_live.rs` with ONE `#[ignore]`-gated test that exercises the full inter-phase end-to-end against a real Anthropic model.

## Required test

`#[tokio::test] #[ignore = "live API, requires ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"]` named something like `builtin_tools_live_read_edit_exec`:

1. **Guard**: If `std::env::var("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY").is_err()`, print a skip message and return early. (Belt-and-suspenders alongside `#[ignore]`.)

2. **Fixture**: Create a unique tempdir via `Uuid::new_v4().simple().to_string()`. Write a file `fixture.py` to it with content `print(1+1)\n`.

3. **Harness construction**: Build a Harness with HarnessBuilder, registering all five tools + AddTool (the same chain as `src/lib.rs::run_async`). Wire it to a real Anthropic ARModel (use the same model name / pattern as the existing exec_bash_smoke or other live tests if there's a precedent — likely `claude-sonnet-4-6` or the latest 4.x family member).

4. **User message**: Send ONE inbound UserMessage:
   `"Read fixture.py (path: <absolute path>), then use the Edit tool to replace '1+1' with '2+2', then use the ExecBash tool to run it with `python3 <absolute path>`. Tell me what number it printed."`

   Pass the absolute path so the model doesn't have to reason about cwd.

5. **Drain**: subscribe to the agent's outbound stream. Collect events until `AgentEvent::Idle`. Use `tokio::time::timeout(Duration::from_secs(60), ...)` to enforce a 60-second cap. On timeout: fail the test with a descriptive message about what events arrived before the timeout (helpful for debugging).

6. **Assertions** (all must hold):
   a. The file's content on disk is now `print(2+2)\n` (assert via `std::fs::read_to_string`).
   b. At least one `BlockComplete` event in the stream has the ExecBash tool name (`"exec_bash"`).
   c. At least one final `AgentMessage` text block contains the substring `"4"` (the result of `2+2`).

   Be lenient with exact message wording: assert *contains* not *equals*. The model may say "4", "four", "The output is 4", etc. We only need the digit "4" present.

   Be strict on file content: it must be exactly `print(2+2)\n`.

## Implementation notes

- The test file lives at `tests/builtin_tools_live.rs`. This is a new integration-test file.
- The test does NOT need to clean up the tempdir; the OS reclaims it.
- DO NOT add unit tests in this file. ONE test only.
- DO NOT use #[ignore] on a test inside `src/builtins/*.rs`. The file lives in `tests/`.
- Use `pristine::` paths to access the public surface (e.g., `pristine::HarnessBuilder`, `pristine::builtins::{Read, Write, Edit, Insert, ExecBash, AddTool}`). Check the actual public re-exports — adjust to match.
- If the existing harness needs a SYSTEM_PROMPT, use whatever pristine's `run_async` uses today (likely re-exposed via a function or constant; otherwise construct manually with a plain identity sentence).
- Read what's currently in `src/lib.rs::run_async` so you mirror the construction. If `run_async` is the only constructor and is not callable as a pub fn directly, you may need to extract a `pub fn default_harness_builder() -> HarnessBuilder` or similar. If you find yourself needing to expose new public API, STOP and report — that is a refactor gate (a separate bead would be needed).

## Constraints

- ONE file added: `tests/builtin_tools_live.rs`.
- ONE test, `#[ignore]`-gated.
- ANTHROPIC_API_KEY guarded.
- Compiles and `cargo nextest run` skips it by default.
- When you have ANTHROPIC_API_KEY set, run `cargo nextest run --run-ignored=only -E 'test(builtin_tools_live)' --no-fail-fast` MANUALLY before reporting done. You should explicitly verify the live test PASSES at least once. If it fails after a reasonable retry (3 attempts), STOP and report — the agent's instruction-following on multi-turn tool calls may be the issue, not the bead's spec.
- If running the live test would expose a refactor gate (need to widen Harness public API), STOP and report.

## Definition of Done

- `cargo fmt --check`
- `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings`
- `cargo nextest run` -- 142 passed, 6 → 7 skipped (one new ignored test added)
- `cargo nextest run --run-ignored=only -E 'test(builtin_tools_live)'` — runs and passes when ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is set. Report the result.
- File: `A tests/builtin_tools_live.rs`. Possibly minor public-surface widening in `src/lib.rs` if needed (report this case).
- Commit has trailer.

## jj workflow

`jj desc -m "$(printf 'Close bd-XXX: EOC-3 chained live integration test (Read -> Edit -> ExecBash)\n\nCo-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>\n')"`

## Plan-respect note

This test is the most concrete demonstration of "Portable shape, adapter dialect": one portable agent run-loop dispatches three different tools (Read, Edit, ExecBash), each speaking its own dialect, all flowing through the portable `ToolError::Execution(Value)` carrier when errors occur and the portable `ContentPart` carrier when results flow. The model treats them uniformly via `ToolSpec` — pure adapter dialect.

title: Built-in tools P-3: Shell trait + BashShell + StubShell
description:
## Context

Prelude bead 3/4 of the Built-in Tools inter-phase. Introduces the `Shell` abstraction that ExecBash will be built on. Unused this bead -- only ExecBash will consume it, in a later bead. Justification this cycle: future shell experimentation, and so ExecBash unit tests can drive a `StubShell` rather than spawning real processes.

## RETRY NOTE (attempt 2)

Attempt 1 was rejected by Judge for a single `expect("...")` call in production code inside `BashShell::exec` (RUST_STYLE_GUIDE.md line 28: "Do not use `unwrap()` / `expect()` in production code"). The implementation otherwise was correct.

The root cause: an intermediate `(Option<ExitStatus>, bool)` tuple flowed through the timeout branch, then was unwrapped with `expect` in the success branch. The fix is to **construct `ExecStatus` directly inside the match arms of the `wait_result`**, so the `ExitStatus` is consumed where it definitely exists, with no `Option` intermediate.

**Concrete shape (sketch)**:

```rust
let exec_status = match wait_result {
    Ok(Ok(exit_status)) => classify_status(exit_status),
    Ok(Err(e)) => return Err(ShellError::Io(format!("{e}"))),
    Err(_elapsed) => {
        let _ = child.kill().await;
        ExecStatus::Timeout
    }
};
```

Where `classify_status` is a private helper that turns an `ExitStatus` into `ExecStatus::Exit { code } | ExecStatus::Signal { name }` per the signal-mapping rules in this bead. Then await the reader tasks to collect captured bytes (the readers naturally drain after the wait/kill).

Equivalent shapes are fine; the constraint is: **no `unwrap()` / `expect()` anywhere in `src/shell.rs` outside `#[cfg(test)]`**, AND no `unwrap()` / `expect()` anywhere in `src/test_support.rs` outside test code. `unwrap_or(...)` / `unwrap_or_else(...)` are fine -- they do not panic.

## Required reading before coding

1. blueprint/builtin-tools/PLAN.md (sections "Decisions" -> "Shell trait", "Type definitions" -> Shell, "P-3")
2. blueprint/builtin-tools/PROMPT.md (the "Shell trait" bullet)
3. src/test_support.rs (look at StubArModel as the template for StubShell)
4. src/tool.rs (note the `jsonrpsee::core::async_trait` precedent -- reuse it; do not add `async-trait` as a new dependency)
5. ARCHITECTURE.md ("Portable shape, adapter dialect" principle)
6. RUST_STYLE_GUIDE.md (the no-unwrap/no-expect rule)

## Goal

Create `src/shell.rs` with the Shell trait and BashShell implementation. Add StubShell to `src/test_support.rs`. Register `pub mod shell;` in `src/lib.rs`. The trait is unused this bead (ExecBash lands later); the file exists so subsequent beads can depend on it. **All production code in the new/modified files must be free of `unwrap()` and `expect()`.**

## Required changes

1. Create `src/shell.rs`:

   ```rust
   use std::time::Duration;

   // Reuse the jsonrpsee re-export to avoid adding a new async-trait dependency.
   use jsonrpsee::core::async_trait;

   #[async_trait]
   pub trait Shell: Send + Sync {
       async fn exec(
           &self,
           command: &str,
           timeout: Duration,
       ) -> Result<ShellOutput, ShellError>;
   }

   pub struct ShellOutput {
       pub stdout: Vec<u8>,
       pub stderr: Vec<u8>,
       pub status: ExecStatus,
   }

   #[derive(Debug, Clone, serde::Serialize)]
   #[serde(tag = "status", rename_all = "snake_case")]
   pub enum ExecStatus {
       Exit { code: i32 },
       Signal { name: String },
       Timeout,
   }

   #[derive(Debug)]
   pub enum ShellError {
       Spawn(String),
       Io(String),
   }
   ```

   Plus `BashShell` (an empty struct with a `pub fn new() -> Self` constructor) implementing `Shell::exec` by:
   - Spawning `/bin/bash -c <command>` via `tokio::process::Command`.
   - Capturing stdout and stderr into in-memory `Vec<u8>` buffers (no 64 KiB tail-truncation here).
   - Enforcing the timeout via `tokio::time::timeout`. On timeout, kill the process and return `Ok(ShellOutput { stdout, stderr, status: ExecStatus::Timeout })` with whatever has been captured so far.
   - On normal exit: `ExecStatus::Exit { code }` from `ExitStatus::code()` (fall back via `unwrap_or(-1)` if `None`, which can occur on Unix when the process was signal-killed -- defensively handle, even though the signal branch usually catches first).
   - On signal kill (Unix only -- `#[cfg(unix)]`): `ExecStatus::Signal { name }` via `std::os::unix::process::ExitStatusExt::signal()`. Map: 1=SIGHUP, 2=SIGINT, 3=SIGQUIT, 9=SIGKILL, 15=SIGTERM. Fall back to `format!("signal-{}", n)`.
   - On spawn failure: `Err(ShellError::Spawn(format!("{e}")))`.
   - On read I/O failure during capture: `Err(ShellError::Io(format!("{e}")))`.

   **Use `classify_status` (or similar) as a helper.** Direct construction of `ExecStatus` inside the wait/timeout match arms. No `Option<ExitStatus>` flow-through. No `expect`/`unwrap` in production code.

2. Register the module in `src/lib.rs`:
   ```rust
   pub mod shell;
   ```
   alongside other module declarations (alphabetical or near `pub mod tool;`).

3. Add `StubShell` to `src/test_support.rs`, mirroring `StubArModel`:
   - `pub struct StubShell { script: std::sync::Mutex<std::collections::VecDeque<Result<ShellOutput, ShellError>>> }`
   - `pub fn new(script: Vec<Result<ShellOutput, ShellError>>) -> Self`
   - `impl Shell for StubShell` that pops the next item from the script. If empty, panic with a clear `panic!("StubShell script exhausted at unexpected exec call: {command}")`. Panics in test_support / tests are acceptable.
   - Use `#[async_trait]` from the same `jsonrpsee::core::async_trait` re-export.
   - Mutex `.lock()` returns a `Result`; handle the poison case without `unwrap()` -- e.g., `let mut script = self.script.lock().unwrap_or_else(|p| p.into_inner());` (this is the standard idiom for recovering from a poisoned mutex without `unwrap()`).

4. Tests:
   - In `src/shell.rs`, add a `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` block with at least TWO `StubShell`-driven tests demonstrating the contract: a successful exit case and a timeout case. These are pure-data tests; they should not spawn processes. Test-code `expect()`/`unwrap()` is fine.
   - Add a SINGLE `#[ignore]`-gated BashShell smoke test (location: inline in `src/shell.rs` `#[cfg(test)] mod tests`, since the existing `tests/*.rs` files are reserved for live-API integration tests). Test: echo a string, assert `ExecStatus::Exit { code: 0 }` and `stdout` contains the bytes.

## Out of scope

- ExecBash itself.
- Per-process tmp directory init.
- 64 KiB tail truncation.
- Any builtins/exec_bash.rs file.

## Definition of Done

- `cargo fmt --check` passes
- `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings` passes
- `cargo nextest run` passes within 120s; ignored test skipped
- **NO `unwrap()` or `expect()` in any production code (non-`#[cfg(test)]`) in the new/modified files.** `unwrap_or` / `unwrap_or_else` are fine.
- No new dependencies in Cargo.toml
- One atomic commit with `Close bd-1cs:` prefix and the standard `Co-Authored-By` trailer

## Plan-respect note

Shell trait is dialect-agnostic: it doesn't know about bash quoting, ExecBash output shape, or tool errors. That is portable shape; the BashShell impl is the adapter dialect. ExecBash (later bead) is the *consumer* that turns Shell output into a tool's JSON dialect.

title: Coordinator: separate bead-state commits from code commits
description:
## Goal
Update `.claude/agents/coordinator.md` to require the Coordinator to commit \`.beads/issues.jsonl\` and \`.beads/last-touched\` state changes as **separate, isolated commits** from any code-bearing change.

## Background
During Phase 1, the Coordinator's pattern has been:
1. `br update <id> --status in_progress`
2. (this dirties `.beads/issues.jsonl` and `.beads/last-touched`)
3. Set up the working-copy description with `jj desc`
4. Delegate to the Coding Subagent
5. The Subagent commits — and its commit now also contains the bead state change

This co-mingles bead state with feature code in the same logical commit, making history harder to read and harder to revert cleanly. It also makes `jj split`/`git reset --hard` on a failed attempt accidentally revert the bead-state transition.

## Required change to `.claude/agents/coordinator.md`
Add a new subsection under "Coordinator workflow" titled **"Bead state commit cadence"** containing:

> When transitioning a bead between statuses (open -> in_progress, in_progress -> done, etc.), the resulting `.beads/issues.jsonl` and `.beads/last-touched` changes MUST be committed as a standalone commit, NOT folded into any code change. Cadence:
> 1. Run `br update <id> --status in_progress`
> 2. Immediately commit ONLY `.beads/issues.jsonl` and `.beads/last-touched` with message `Mark <id> in_progress`. Use `jj new -m "Mark <id> in_progress" .beads/`.
> 3. Then create a fresh empty commit for the Subagent to work in: `jj new -m "<task title>"`.
> 4. Delegate to the Coding Subagent.
> 5. After Judge passes, run `br update <id> --status done` and commit ONLY the bead state files with message `Mark <id> done`.
>
> Rationale: keeps task-tracking changes orthogonal to code changes; makes `jj abandon` / `git reset` on a failed attempt safe (it reverts the code without losing the in_progress marker, which the Coordinator can use to detect the retry).

## Files that may change
- `.claude/agents/coordinator.md`

## Files that must NOT change
- Any other agent file
- Any source code

## Verification
- The new subsection is present and well-formed
- `git diff` shows ONLY changes to `.claude/agents/coordinator.md`
- (No build verification needed; doc-only change)

## Out of scope
- Updating Coding Subagent or Judge Subagent docs (those agents do not manage bead state)
- Retroactively splitting past commits

title: ExecBash T-1: types (input, output, error enums) in src/builtins/exec_bash.rs
description:
## Context

First of four ExecBash slices. Lands the type vocabulary for the ExecBash tool. No `Tool` trait impl yet (that is T-2). No tests yet (those are T-3). No registration yet (T-4).

ExecBash is the highest-risk tool in the inter-phase. The plan orders it first so that issues surface early. Splitting types from impl means a clean diff for each.

## Required reading before coding

1. blueprint/builtin-tools/PLAN.md (sections "Type definitions" -> Per-tool input shapes / Per-tool error enums / ExecBash output / "ExecBash" subsection of "Per-tool behavioral specifications" / "T-1. Types.")
2. blueprint/builtin-tools/PROMPT.md (the "ExecBash" bullet)
3. src/builtins.rs (current aggregator after bd-21u)
4. src/builtins/add.rs (the template: the per-tool err helper + AddError pattern)
5. src/shell.rs (the `ExecStatus` enum that ExecBash will re-use)
6. ARCHITECTURE.md ("Portable shape, adapter dialect"; the new "## Built-in Tools" section with ExecBash stub)
7. RUST_STYLE_GUIDE.md (no unwrap/expect in production; non-mod-rs; no banner comments)

## Goal

Create `src/builtins/exec_bash.rs` containing only the TYPE vocabulary for the ExecBash tool. Register the submodule in `src/builtins.rs`. No `Tool` impl, no behavior.

## Required deliverables

1. **Create `src/builtins/exec_bash.rs`** with the following items, in this order:

   ```rust
   use crate::shell::ExecStatus;
   use crate::tool::ToolError;

   #[derive(serde::Deserialize)]
   struct ExecBashInput {
       command: String,
       timeout_seconds: Option<u64>,
   }

   #[derive(serde::Serialize)]
   #[serde(tag = "kind", rename_all = "snake_case")]
   enum ExecBashError {
       Spawn { reason: String },
       Io { reason: String },
       TmpFile { reason: String },
   }

   fn err(e: ExecBashError) -> ToolError {
       let value = serde_json::to_value(e)
           .unwrap_or_else(|_| serde_json::json!({"kind": "internal_error"}));
       ToolError::Execution(value)
   }

   #[derive(serde::Serialize)]
   struct ExecBashOutput {
       stdout: String,
       stderr: String,
       status: ExecStatus,
       stdout_truncated: bool,
       stderr_truncated: bool,
       has_invalid_utf8_stdout: bool,
       has_invalid_utf8_stderr: bool,
       execution_id: String,
   }

   pub struct ExecBash;

   impl ExecBash {
       pub fn new() -> Self {
           ExecBash
       }
   }
   ```

   Notes:
   - All types except `ExecBash` itself are private to the module (no `pub` on Input, Error, Output, or err). The `Tool` impl (in T-2) will be the only consumer.
   - The `err` helper mirrors the AddError pattern from `src/builtins/add.rs`. Use `unwrap_or_else`, not `unwrap`/`expect`.
   - `ExecBash` has only `new() -> Self`. No `Default` impl yet (it can be added in T-2 if clippy demands).

2. **Update `src/builtins.rs`** to register the new submodule and re-export the tool:

   ```rust
   //! ...existing doc...

   pub mod add;
   pub mod exec_bash;

   pub use add::AddTool;
   pub use exec_bash::ExecBash;
   ```

3. **Dead-code allowances**:
   - Because nothing uses `ExecBashInput`, `ExecBashError`, `ExecBashOutput`, or `err` in this bead, clippy/rustc will likely complain about unused items. Add `#[allow(dead_code)]` at the module-top (`#![allow(dead_code)]` is too broad — use targeted `#[allow(dead_code)]` on EACH of: the enum, the input struct, the output struct, and the `err` function). Do NOT use `#[allow(dead_code)]` on the `ExecBash` struct or its `new()` -- those are re-exported and thus considered used.

4. **No changes to `Cargo.toml`.** The uuid crate is NOT needed in this bead (execution_id is generated in T-2).

## Out of scope

- ANY `Tool` impl for `ExecBash`. That is T-2.
- Tests of any kind. Those are T-3.
- Registration in `HarnessBuilder` / `src/lib.rs::run_async`. That is T-4.
- ARCHITECTURE.md update (the ExecBash subsection stays a stub until T-4).
- Any actual shell invocation, tmp file creation, UUID generation.

## Definition of Done

- `cargo fmt --check`
- `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings` (with the `#[allow(dead_code)]` attrs in place)
- `cargo nextest run` -- test count unchanged from baseline (93 passed, 3 skipped)
- `jj diff --summary` shows exactly: `M src/builtins.rs`, `A src/builtins/exec_bash.rs`.
- One atomic commit with `Close bd-XXX: ExecBash T-1 (types)` (the bead ID will be assigned). Trailer per project convention.

## Plan-respect note

`ExecBashError` is the ExecBash dialect: its variants (`Spawn`/`Io`/`TmpFile`) are tool-specific. The `err` helper bridges to the portable carrier `ToolError::Execution(Value)`. `ExecStatus` is shared with `Shell` because it is a *Shell-level* concept (exit/signal/timeout), not an ExecBash dialect specialization — re-using it here is the principle's "promotion" rule in action.

description:

title: Phase C3: Parse-don't-validate error collection across both files
description:
Plan: blueprint/configuration-file/plan-configuration-file.md (Phase C3).

Tie B3 (templating errors), C1 (alias errors), and C2 (tool-ref errors) into a single ConfigErrors aggregate. The loader runs every pass it can — even if one pass collected errors — and returns Err(ConfigErrors) with the full collection if any errors were found. Only on a fully empty error set does it return Ok(Config).

Constraints:
- Parse errors (Phase B2 toml::de::Error) are fatal per-file: if the topology file fails to parse, we cannot run later passes on its tree, so we still collect the auth file's errors but skip topology-dependent passes (alias / tool-ref) for that file.
- ConfigErrors implements Display showing every error with file path + (where available) line/column info.
- ConfigErrors implements std::error::Error.

Unit tests: a single load that surfaces multiple distinct errors across both files (e.g. one TomlParse on auth + one UndeclaredTool on topology + one UnknownEnvVar) — assert the returned ConfigErrors contains all three.

Files to read: src/config/ from B and C1-C2.

Definition of Done: cargo fmt --check, cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features, cargo nextest run all pass.

title: Phase 1 Step 2: History and Block
description:
## Goal
Implement the persistent-history primitives: `NodeId`, the `Block` enum, and a `History` linked-list/stack built on `Arc<HistoryNode>`. This is pure data plumbing; no async, no model, no agent yet.

## Reference docs
- `ARCHITECTURE.md` §History (authoritative)
- `blueprint/pristine-phase1-architecture/plan.md` §"Step 2: History and Block" and §"`NodeId`"
- `STYLE_GUIDE.md` (no `unwrap`/`expect` in production; newtypes over primitives; no `mod.rs`)

## Implement in `src/history.rs`
- `pub struct NodeId(uuid::Uuid)` — UUID newtype with `pub fn new() -> Self` (v4) and a `Display` impl. Derive `Clone, Copy, Debug, PartialEq, Eq, Hash`.
- `pub struct AgentId(uuid::Uuid)` and `pub struct UserId(uuid::Uuid)` — same newtype shape as `NodeId`. (Step 6 will expose `UserId`/Owner via Harness; Step 7 will wire `AgentId` from `AgentBuilder`. Define both here so `Block` variants type-check.)
- `pub enum Block` with the variants per ARCHITECTURE.md §History:
  - `UserMessage { from: UserId, content: String, timestamp: std::time::SystemTime }`
  - `ReasoningTrace { content: String, timestamp: std::time::SystemTime }`
  - `ToolCall { name: String, arguments: serde_json::Value, timestamp: std::time::SystemTime }`
  - `ToolResult { name: String, result: serde_json::Value, timestamp: std::time::SystemTime }`
  - `AgentMessage { from: AgentId, content: String, timestamp: std::time::SystemTime }`
  - Derive `Clone, Debug`. Do NOT derive `PartialEq` (avoids painful trait bounds on `serde_json::Value`).
- `pub struct HistoryNode { id: NodeId, timestamp: std::time::SystemTime, block: Block, parent: Option<Arc<HistoryNode>> }`
  - All fields private; provide accessors `id()`, `timestamp()`, `block() -> &Block`, `parent() -> Option<&Arc<HistoryNode>>`.
- `pub struct History { head: Option<Arc<HistoryNode>> }`
  - `pub fn new() -> Self` (empty history)
  - `pub fn append(&mut self, block: Block) -> Arc<HistoryNode>` — creates a new node parented to `head`, advances head, returns the new node Arc.
  - `pub fn head(&self) -> Option<&Arc<HistoryNode>>`
  - `pub fn linearize(&self) -> Vec<Block>` — walks from head to root collecting `Block` clones, then reverses so the result is root-first/oldest-first.
- `pub enum Error { /* keep empty for now: future variants will land when ops can fail */ }` (or omit the enum entirely if clippy objects; the wider crate `Error` does not yet reference `history::Error`).

## Unit tests (in the same file under `#[cfg(test)] mod tests`)
- `append_advances_head_and_links_parent`: after two appends, the head's parent points to the first node.
- `linearize_returns_root_first`: append three blocks, assert `linearize()` returns them in append order.
- `fork_shares_prefix_via_arc`: construct one history, then clone it (or clone its head) and append different blocks to each branch; verify both branches' linearizations share the common prefix and the original `Arc` count proves sharing (`Arc::strong_count` on the shared node is > 1).

Use `unwrap()` freely inside tests (per STYLE_GUIDE.md: acceptable in tests).

## Wiring
- Declare `pub mod history;` in `src/lib.rs` (the Step 1 Bead may already have done this).
- Do NOT add `history::Error` to the crate-root `Error` enum unless it exists.

## Definition of Done
- `cargo fmt --check`, `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings`, `cargo nextest run` all pass.
- No STYLE_GUIDE.md violations.

## Out of scope
- Persistence / `HistoryStore`
- Any async code
- `prepare_for_completion` shaping (Step 7 deals with linearization in context of an Agent)

title: Remove unused ToolKind and ProviderKind type aliases from src/config
description:
## Problem

`src/config/topology.rs:50` declares `pub type ToolKind = ToolConfig;` and `src/config/auth.rs:41` declares `pub type ProviderKind = ProviderConfig;`. Both are re-exported from `src/config.rs`.

`grep` shows zero call sites:

```
$ grep -rn 'ToolKind' src/ tests/
src/config/topology.rs:38:/// `ToolConfig` and `ToolKind` are intentionally the same type: ...
src/config/topology.rs:50:pub type ToolKind = ToolConfig;
src/config.rs:32:pub use topology::{AgentConfig, ToolConfig, ToolKind, TopologyConfig};

$ grep -rn 'ProviderKind' src/ tests/
src/config/auth.rs:28:/// `ProviderConfig` and `ProviderKind` are intentionally the same type: ...
src/config/auth.rs:41:pub type ProviderKind = ProviderConfig;
src/config.rs:24:pub use auth::{AuthConfig, ModelAliasConfig, ProviderConfig, ProviderKind};
```

These aliases were added as a 'placeholder for downstream beads to name the variant set explicitly'. Phases C1 (`bd-242`) and C2 (`bd-ev8`) are now complete; `assemble_config` (`bd-1qb`) is complete; nothing in the landed code or in any open bead's scope references the alias names. Per RUST_STYLE_GUIDE.md ('Keep implementation details private by default') and ARCHITECTURE.md ('The portable layer never grows speculatively'), unused `pub` items should not linger.

## Scope (single commit)

1. Delete `pub type ToolKind = ToolConfig;` from `src/config/topology.rs` and the surrounding 3-line comment block ('Alias retained so downstream beads can name the variant set explicitly.').
2. Delete `pub type ProviderKind = ProviderConfig;` from `src/config/auth.rs` and its 3-line comment block.
3. Remove `ToolKind` from the `pub use` re-export in `src/config.rs:32`.
4. Remove `ProviderKind` from the `pub use` re-export in `src/config.rs:24`.
5. The pre-existing block comments on `ToolConfig` / `ProviderConfig` that explain 'serde's flatten is incompatible with deny_unknown_fields' should be retained (load-bearing for future maintainers); only the 'and `ToolKind` are intentionally the same type' phrasing needs to be reworded so it does not mention the deleted alias.

## Out of scope

- Renaming `ToolConfig` / `ProviderConfig` themselves.
- Any other dead-code sweeps.

## Definition of done

- `cargo fmt --check` passes.
- `cargo clippy` passes with no warnings.
- `cargo nextest run` passes.
- No `ToolKind` or `ProviderKind` identifiers remain anywhere in `src/` or `tests/`.

Remove the struct and its derive. The struct also has a minor style inconsistency: it uses fully-qualified serde::Deserialize while Deserialize is already imported on line 6.

title: Phase 1 Step 4: Anthropic ARModel
description:
## Goal
Implement `AnthropicModel` — the first concrete `ARModel`. Uses the Anthropic Messages API with `stream: true` over SSE.

## Reference docs
- `ARCHITECTURE.md` §Model (authoritative)
- `blueprint/pristine-phase1-architecture/plan.md` §"Step 4: Anthropic ARModel"
- `STYLE_GUIDE.md` (no `unwrap`/`expect` in production; typed errors; private API types stay private)

## Implement in `src/model/anthropic.rs`
- `pub struct AnthropicModel { client: reqwest::Client, api_key: String, model_name: String, base_url: String }`
  - All fields private. The `base_url` defaults to `https://api.anthropic.com` and exists so tests can point at a mock server later (no test uses it in Phase 1, but the seam is cheap).
- `pub struct AnthropicModelBuilder { api_key: Option<String>, model_name: Option<String>, base_url: Option<String> }`
  - `pub fn new() -> Self`
  - `pub fn api_key(mut self, key: impl Into<String>) -> Self`
  - `pub fn model_name(mut self, name: impl Into<String>) -> Self`
  - `pub fn base_url(mut self, url: impl Into<String>) -> Self`
  - `pub fn build(self) -> Result<AnthropicModel, super::Error>` — returns `model::Error::Configuration(...)` if `api_key` or `model_name` is missing. Constructs the `reqwest::Client` here.
- `impl ARModel for AnthropicModel` — `complete(&self, system_prompt, messages)` returns a pinned boxed stream.

### Request shape (private types in this file only)
Define `#[derive(serde::Serialize)] struct AnthropicRequest<'a> { model: &'a str, max_tokens: u32, stream: bool, system: &'a str, messages: Vec<AnthropicMessage> }` and `#[derive(serde::Serialize)] struct AnthropicMessage { role: &'static str, content: String }`. Translate `Block` variants:
- `Block::UserMessage { content, .. }` → `role: "user"`, content cloned.
- `Block::AgentMessage { content, .. }` → `role: "assistant"`, content cloned.
- Other variants are skipped in Phase 1 (Anthropic Messages API has no slot for them yet; `ReasoningTrace`/`ToolCall`/`ToolResult` are reserved for later phases).

Hard-code `max_tokens = 1024` in Phase 1; document the choice in a single-line comment.

Required headers on every POST:
- `x-api-key: <api_key>`
- `anthropic-version: 2023-06-01`
- `content-type: application/json`

POST URL: `{base_url}/v1/messages`.

### Response handling
Use `eventsource-stream` to parse the SSE byte stream from `reqwest`'s streaming body. For each event, dispatch on the SSE `event:` name:
- `message_start` → parse `{"message": {"id": ..., "model": ..., "usage": {"input_tokens": ...}}}` → emit `ModelStreamEvent::MessageStart { message_id, model }` and an initial `ModelStreamEvent::Usage(Usage { input_tokens, output_tokens: 0 })`.
- `content_block_delta` → `{"delta": {"type": "text_delta", "text": "..."}}` → emit `ModelStreamEvent::ContentDelta { text }`.
- `content_block_stop` → emit `ModelStreamEvent::ContentComplete { text: accumulated_so_far }`. Accumulate text per content block; reset on `content_block_start`.
- `message_delta` → `{"usage": {"output_tokens": ...}}` → emit `ModelStreamEvent::Usage(...)` with the cumulative counts. Hold the latest seen usage for the final `MessageComplete`.
- `message_stop` → emit `ModelStreamEvent::MessageComplete { usage }` using the last cumulative usage.
- `ping`, `content_block_start` → no-ops (do not emit anything).
- Any deserialization failure → yield a `model::Error::Deserialization` once and end the stream.

If the HTTP status is non-2xx, the first item yielded from the stream is `Err(model::Error::Api { status, message: body })` and the stream ends. Do NOT panic.

Use `async_stream::try_stream!` if `async-stream` is added as a dep — but plan.md did not approve it. Prefer hand-rolling the stream with `futures::stream::unfold` or `tokio_stream`. Document any new dep request as a FAIL with reasoning rather than silently adding one.

## Implement `From<reqwest::Error> for model::Error`
In `src/model/anthropic.rs` (so `src/model.rs` stays reqwest-free):
- Map to `model::Error::Http(err.to_string())`.

Same trick for any `serde_json::Error` → `model::Error::Deserialization(err.to_string())`.

## Tests
- A unit test that `AnthropicModelBuilder::new().build()` returns `Err(Configuration)`.
- A unit test that `AnthropicModel: Send + Sync`.
- A unit test that builds a request body for a small `[Block::UserMessage]` and asserts the serialized JSON contains the expected `messages` shape (use `serde_json::to_value` and assert on specific fields).
- One `#[ignore]`-gated integration test in `tests/anthropic_live.rs` (NEW file, must declare `use pristine::...`) that:
  - Reads `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` from env; if missing, `eprintln!("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY not set; skipping")` and `return` early (do not fail).
  - Builds an `AnthropicModel` with `model_name = "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001"`.
  - Sends a tiny `UserMessage` ("ping") and asserts at least one `ContentDelta` is received.
  - Marked `#[tokio::test]` `#[ignore]` so `cargo nextest run` skips it by default; a developer runs it with `cargo nextest run --ignored anthropic_live`.

## Definition of Done
- `cargo fmt --check`, `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings`, `cargo nextest run` all pass.
- The live test is `#[ignore]`d and not run by default.
- No STYLE_GUIDE.md violations.

## Out of scope
- Tool use, vision, JSON mode, prompt caching
- `Block::ReasoningTrace` / `ToolCall` / `ToolResult` translation (they are skipped, not errored on)
- Mock-server tests (the seam is left via `base_url`; populating it is a later Bead)

title: Phase 2 Step 7b: Agent run loop dispatches tool calls and re-enters model
Remove dead model::Error::Configuration variant
description:
## Problem
`crate::model::Error::Configuration(String)` has no constructors anywhere in the codebase. Verified via `grep -rn "Configuration" src/ tests/`:
- src/model.rs:63 — variant declaration
- src/model.rs:72 — Display match arm

The Judge for bd-3lw flagged this when `AnthropicModelBuilder` (the variant's only user) was replaced by `AnthropicProvider`. Clippy does not warn because the variant is on a `pub` enum.

## Why now
Dead variants on a public enum widen the API surface for no benefit, give future readers a misleading hint that the engine reports configuration errors through `model::Error` (in fact `provider::ProviderError::BuildFailure` is the live channel), and force every exhaustive `match` over `model::Error` to keep a no-op arm.

Distinct from `agent::Error::Configuration` (separate enum, in active use in `AgentBuilder::build`). This bead targets only the variant in `src/model.rs`.

## Acceptance criteria
- `model::Error::Configuration` is removed from `src/model.rs`.
- The matching arm in the `Display` impl is removed.
- `cargo fmt --check`, `cargo clippy`, and `cargo nextest run` pass.
- No other call sites need updating (grep confirms zero constructors today).

## Out of scope
- `agent::Error::Configuration` (in use; do not touch).
- Any consolidation between `provider::ProviderError` and `model::Error`.

But topology has no default file path — the default behavior is to use the embedded default.toml, which has no path. So resolve_topology_path is None when no override is given.
