title: Redesign MessageBus trait for cross-process implementability
Process: Clarify Coordinator retry policy for cosmetic vs substantive Judge FAILs
description:
PROBLEM (observed on bd-ike / Phase A3):
The Coding Subagent shipped a passing implementation but omitted the mandatory
Co-Authored-By trailer on the close commit. Judge correctly FAILed. The
Coordinator then applied a metadata-only fix (`jj desc -m ...`) to amend the
description, rather than following the documented protocol (reset to
pre-attempt commit + fresh Coding Subagent + amended bead).

This is ambiguous in `.claude/agents/coordinator.md`. The retry section says:
- "Run `git reset --hard` to revert to pre-attempt commit."
- "Retry with a fresh Coding Subagent (max 4 attempts total)."
It does NOT carve out a category for Judge FAILs that are purely metadata
defects on an otherwise-correct content commit. The Coordinator made a
judgment call; that call may or may not be the intended policy.

WHY THIS IS PROCESS-IMPROVING:
The retry protocol exists to keep diffs clean and the Judge honest. If
Coordinators silently downgrade "FAIL" to "patch the metadata," the retry-cap
(4 attempts) and the bead-amendment loop (which is how failure modes get
folded back into future bead specs) both lose force. Conversely, if the
literal policy is followed for trailer-only defects, we burn a Coding Subagent
context window to rewrite an identical content commit. Either choice is
defensible; the agent docs must pick one and say so.

PROPOSED CHANGE (touches `.claude/agents/coordinator.md`):
Add a subsection under "If Judge fails" enumerating cases where a metadata-
only fix on `@` (e.g. trailer missing, description typo) is permitted WITHOUT
a reset+retry, AND state that any such fix:
  - does not count against the 4-attempt cap,
  - must be re-judged before close,
  - must be logged in the Coordinator's bead-close summary.
For anything touching the diff itself (code, tests, file structure), the
existing reset+retry protocol stands unchanged.

ALSO: replace the literal `git reset --hard` references in coordinator.md
with the jj equivalent (`jj abandon @` or `jj restore .`), since this repo
uses jj, not git.

ACCEPTANCE:
- coordinator.md "If Judge fails" section distinguishes metadata-only fixes
  from substantive fixes, with explicit examples and constraints.
- All `git reset --hard` mentions in coordinator.md replaced with jj
  equivalents.
- Single atomic commit on a fresh `@`, with trailer.

title: Built-in tools: convert src/builtins.rs to non-mod-rs aggregator
description:
## Context

Bridge bead between the Prelude and the per-tool slices. Today `src/builtins.rs` is a flat file containing `AddTool`, `AddError`, the `err` helper, and the unit tests. The per-tool slices need the file to be a non-mod-rs aggregator pointing at `src/builtins/{name}.rs` submodules.

This bead is a pure refactor: zero behavior change, zero test changes, layout-only.

## Required reading

1. blueprint/builtin-tools/PLAN.md (the "Module layout" section)
2. RUST_STYLE_GUIDE.md (the "Module layout" section: non-mod-rs is required for new code)
3. src/builtins.rs (current flat layout)
4. src/lib.rs (verify how the builtins module is declared)

## Goal

Convert `src/builtins.rs` from a single-file module into a non-mod-rs aggregator with `AddTool` and friends relocated to `src/builtins/add.rs`. All tests and call sites continue to work unchanged.

## Required changes

1. Create directory `src/builtins/` (it does not exist yet).

2. Create `src/builtins/add.rs` and MOVE the following from `src/builtins.rs` into it (verbatim, preserving order, no behavioral edits):
   - The `AddError` enum.
   - The `err(AddError) -> ToolError` helper.
   - The `AddTool` struct and its `Tool` trait impl.
   - The `AddTool::new()` and any `Default` impl.
   - The `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` block (or whatever the existing test structure is).

3. Replace the contents of `src/builtins.rs` with the aggregator form:
   ```rust
   pub mod add;

   pub use add::AddTool;
   ```
   (Match the existing license/header style if one exists. Per RUST_STYLE_GUIDE.md, no banner comments.)

4. Verify that `src/lib.rs` still has the same `pub mod builtins;` declaration. Do NOT add a new declaration.

5. Verify that no other file in `src/` references `crate::builtins::AddTool` (or similar) with a path that breaks. The `pub use add::AddTool;` re-export preserves the existing import path.

## Out of scope

- Adding new tools, new error variants, new derives, or any new behavior.
- Touching `Cargo.toml`.
- Renaming anything.
- Reformatting moved code beyond what `cargo fmt` does automatically.

## Definition of Done

- `cargo fmt --check`
- `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings`
- `cargo nextest run` passes within 120s; the test count is unchanged (current: 93 run, 3 skipped).
- `git diff` shows ONLY: `D` then `A` for `src/builtins.rs` (or `M` if jj treats it as a modify), and `A` for `src/builtins/add.rs`. No other files modified.
- One atomic commit, `Close bd-XXX: convert src/builtins.rs to non-mod-rs aggregator` (the bead ID will be assigned at creation). Trailer per project convention.

## Plan-respect note

This is structural plumbing for the per-tool phase. The "Portable shape, adapter dialect" principle is unaffected — every existing carrier (ToolError, ToolSpec, Tool trait) stays exactly the same.

title: Fix dual [[bin]] manifest warning in Cargo.toml
description:
## Goal
Eliminate the cargo build warning:

    warning: file `/home/nvader/projects/pristine/src/bin/pristine.rs` found to be present in multiple build targets:
      * `bin` target `1p`
      * `bin` target `pristine`

This warning is emitted at the manifest level (cargo, not rustc), so it is NOT caught by `-D warnings` and has been silently accruing. Multiple Coding Subagents flagged it during Phase 1.

## Context
`Cargo.toml` currently declares two `[[bin]]` targets pointing at the same `src/bin/pristine.rs` file. The intent is to ship the binary under two names (`pristine` and `1p`), but Cargo does not support multi-naming via duplicate bin targets.

## Required change
Pick ONE of these resolutions and document the choice in a one-line comment in `Cargo.toml`:

1. Single canonical bin + post-install alias: Keep only the `pristine` `[[bin]]`. Drop the `1p` entry. `1p` can be provided via a justfile recipe or a tiny shim binary if needed.

2. Dedicated shim bin: Keep `pristine` canonical, and create a NEW `src/bin/1p.rs` that is one line: `fn main() { pristine::main_impl() }` (requires factoring `main` into `lib.rs` as `pub fn main_impl()`). This is more invasive.

Recommendation: choose option 1 unless the user has previously requested otherwise. The blueprint references `1p` only as a convenience alias.

## Files that may change
- `Cargo.toml`
- (option 2 only) `src/lib.rs`, `src/bin/1p.rs`

## Files that must NOT change
- `src/bin/pristine.rs` (content is owned by Step 8 / bd-2y6)
- Any other `src/*.rs`

## Verification
- `cargo build 2>&1 | grep -i warning` is empty
- `cargo fmt --check`, `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings`, `cargo nextest run` all pass
- `cargo build --release --bin pristine` succeeds

## Out of scope
- Any change to binary behavior
- Rewriting the binary (that belongs to bd-2y6)

description:

title: Coordinator: document transient Judge-failure protocol (no verdict rendered)
description:
## Why

During the most recent batch (Phase C), a Judge invocation on bd-1qb crashed mid-review with a transient socket/API error. The Coordinator improvised — re-ran the Judge from scratch with the same prompt — and got a clean PASS. This worked, but the protocol is undocumented and currently conflates two very different failure modes:

1. **Substantive FAIL** — Judge ran to completion and rendered FAIL. Diff has a real problem. Triggers `jj abandon @`, counts against the 4-attempt cap, requires re-prompting the Coding Subagent.
2. **Infrastructure failure** — Judge tooling crashed (socket error, API timeout, subagent failed to return). No verdict was rendered. Diff is untouched and may be entirely fine.

Today both are handled by the same "If Judge fails" block in coordinator.md (lines 42-52). That block assumes a verdict was rendered. The Coordinator currently has no documented rule for case 2.

## Concrete observation

bd-1qb (assemble_config orchestrator). First Judge invocation crashed with a socket error before a verdict was emitted. Coordinator re-ran the same Judge prompt; it PASSed cleanly. Implicitly the Coordinator made the right call (do not abandon `@`, do not count the crash against retries), but nothing in coordinator.md authorizes this.

## What to change

Edit `.claude/agents/coordinator.md`. In the "If Judge fails" block, add a new clause distinguishing **transient Judge failure** (no verdict rendered, tooling crashed) from **substantive Judge FAIL** (verdict rendered):

> **Transient Judge failure exception.** If the Judge Subagent fails before rendering a verdict — Task tool returns an error, agent crashes, socket/API timeout, or the agent's final message contains no PASS/FAIL — this is NOT a substantive FAIL. The Coordinator MUST:
> - Leave `@` untouched (do NOT run `jj abandon @`).
> - Re-run the Judge Subagent with the same prompt.
> - NOT count the crash against the 4-attempt cap.
> - If the Judge crashes three times in a row, escalate for human input (Judge tooling itself may be broken).
>
> A transient Judge failure means no verdict was rendered. If the Judge rendered FAIL — even briefly, even with a partial reason — that is a substantive FAIL and follows the abandon-and-retry protocol.

## Acceptance

- `.claude/agents/coordinator.md` has the transient-Judge-failure clause in the "If Judge fails" block.
- The clause is placed near the existing metadata-only-fix exception, since both are exceptions to the default abandon-and-retry protocol.
- No other agent files changed.

title: Phase 2 Step 5b: Anthropic adapter: serialize tools and tool content blocks in request
description:
Refactor the Anthropic adapter's outgoing-request serialization so it can carry tool definitions and tool-use/tool-result content blocks. The streaming-response parser (parsing tool_use deltas back from Anthropic) is a SEPARATE bead -- this bead is request-side only.

Background (read carefully before editing):
- Currently src/model/anthropic.rs uses `AnthropicMessage { role: &'static str, content: String }` -- the API also accepts this flat string form. The new requirement is to support content blocks of type `tool_use` and `tool_result`. The simplest path is to always serialize content as an array of typed content blocks (Anthropic accepts arrays for all roles).
- ModelInput now has a `tools: Vec<ToolSpec>` field (added by bd-3e0). When non-empty, it must be serialized into a top-level `tools` field on the request body. The shape Anthropic expects is:
  `{"name": "...", "description": "...", "input_schema": {...}}` per tool.
- Tool-use serialization (assistant role): `{"type": "tool_use", "id": "...", "name": "...", "input": {...}}`.
- Tool-result serialization (user role): `{"type": "tool_result", "tool_use_id": "...", "content": ..., "is_error": ...}`.

Scope -- src/model/anthropic.rs only:

1. Refactor the outgoing-request types to use typed content blocks:
   - Introduce new `#[derive(serde::Serialize)] enum AnthropicContentBlock` with variants tagged via `#[serde(tag = "type", rename_all = "snake_case")]`:
     ```rust
     enum AnthropicContentBlock {
         Text { text: String },
         ToolUse { id: String, name: String, input: serde_json::Value },
         ToolResult {
             tool_use_id: String,
             content: serde_json::Value,
             #[serde(default, skip_serializing_if = "std::ops::Not::not")]
             is_error: bool,
         },
     }
     ```
     The `is_error` field is skipped when false (Anthropic defaults to false). Use a serde-compatible idiom; if `std::ops::Not::not` doesn't compile cleanly as a serde skip-predicate, write a tiny `fn is_false(b: &bool) -> bool { !*b }` helper.
   - Update `struct AnthropicMessage` so `content` becomes `Vec<AnthropicContentBlock>`. Update the `serde::Serialize` derive accordingly.

2. Introduce serializable tool descriptor:
   ```rust
   #[derive(serde::Serialize)]
   struct AnthropicTool {
       name: String,
       description: String,
       input_schema: serde_json::Value,
   }
   ```
   And add `tools: Vec<AnthropicTool>` to `struct AnthropicRequest`, with `#[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Vec::is_empty")]` so the field is omitted when there are no tools (preserves the Phase 1 request shape exactly when no tools are configured -- important for the existing tests).

3. Refactor `model_input_to_anthropic(input: &ModelInput) -> (String, Vec<AnthropicMessage>, Vec<AnthropicTool>)`:
   - The system string is unchanged.
   - For each Role::User and Role::Assistant turn, build a Vec<AnthropicContentBlock> by mapping each ContentPart:
     - `ContentPart::Text(t)` -> `AnthropicContentBlock::Text { text: t.clone() }`
     - `ContentPart::ToolUse { id, name, input }` -> `AnthropicContentBlock::ToolUse { id: id.clone(), name: name.clone(), input: input.clone() }` (assistant-role only per Anthropic's protocol, but emit in any role -- correctness is the caller's responsibility)
     - `ContentPart::ToolResult { tool_use_id, content, is_error }` -> `AnthropicContentBlock::ToolResult { tool_use_id: tool_use_id.clone(), content: content.clone(), is_error: *is_error }` (user-role only per Anthropic's protocol)
   - For each `ToolSpec` in `input.tools`, build an `AnthropicTool { name: spec.name.clone(), description: spec.description.clone(), input_schema: spec.input_schema.clone() }`.

4. Update the call site in `impl ARModel for AnthropicModel::complete`:
   - Capture the third return value: `let (system, messages, tools) = model_input_to_anthropic(input);`
   - Pass `tools` into `AnthropicRequest`.

5. Remove the two existing no-op match arms in `model_input_to_anthropic` for `ContentPart::ToolUse`/`ContentPart::ToolResult` since they are now handled. Replace with real serialization.

Tests in src/model/anthropic.rs `mod tests`:

- KEEP existing tests `serializes_user_messages_correctly` and `system_turns_concatenated_into_system_field`. Update them to assert that `content` is now an array form: `value["messages"][0]["content"][0]["type"] == "text"` and `value["messages"][0]["content"][0]["text"] == "hello"`. Confirm the `tools` field is absent from the JSON when `input.tools` is empty (use `value.get("tools").is_none()`).
- ADD `serializes_tools_field_when_present`: ModelInput with a single ToolSpec `{name:"echo", description:"d", input_schema: json!({"type":"object"})}`. Serialize the request body. Assert `value["tools"][0]["name"] == "echo"`, `value["tools"][0]["input_schema"]["type"] == "object"`.
- ADD `serializes_tool_use_content_block`: ModelInput with an Assistant Turn carrying `ContentPart::ToolUse { id: "use_1", name: "echo", input: json!({"text":"hi"}) }`. Assert `value["messages"][0]["role"] == "assistant"`, `value["messages"][0]["content"][0]["type"] == "tool_use"`, and id/name/input round-trip.
- ADD `serializes_tool_result_content_block`: ModelInput with a User Turn carrying `ContentPart::ToolResult { tool_use_id: "use_1", content: json!("ok"), is_error: false }`. Assert `value["messages"][0]["content"][0]["type"] == "tool_result"`, `tool_use_id` round-trips, and `is_error` is ABSENT (skip-when-false invariant). Construct a second case with `is_error: true` and assert the field appears as true.
- ADD `serializes_mixed_text_and_tool_use_blocks`: An assistant turn whose content is `[Text("thinking..."), ToolUse{...}]`; assert both blocks appear in the array in order.

Out of scope -- DO NOT DO:
- Parse tool_use events from the streaming response (bd-5c, next bead).
- Touch src/model.rs, src/agent.rs, src/tool.rs, src/history.rs, src/harness.rs, src/messagebus.rs, src/stdio.rs, src/rpc.rs.
- Wire a ToolRegistry into the Agent or Harness (separate bead).
- Update DESIGN.md or ARCHITECTURE.md.
- Add dependencies (serde is already in tree).

Constraints:
- No new dependencies.
- No `unwrap`/`expect` outside `#[cfg(test)]`.
- RUST_STYLE_GUIDE.md: no banner comments, no decorative dividers, no usage examples in doc-comments.
- The Phase 1 request shape for tool-free calls MUST be preserved: when `input.tools` is empty and there are no tool content parts, the serialized request body must NOT contain a `tools` key. (Existing tests verify shape-by-shape; new tests add the `tools` field check.)
- Avoid backward-compat shims: change the AnthropicMessage type fully -- do not keep the old String field alongside the new Vec.

Definition of Done:
- `cargo fmt --check`
- `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features` (zero warnings)
- `cargo nextest run` (within 120s)

Commit policy: one atomic commit. Do NOT modify .beads/issues.jsonl or .beads/last-touched. Use the trailer `Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>`.

title: Tidy: align StubShell lock-poisoning behavior with StubArModel
description:
## Goal

Make `StubShell` and `StubArModel` use the same lock-poisoning idiom in `src/test_support.rs`. Today they diverge:

- `StubArModel` uses `.lock().expect("test lock")` (3 call sites at lines ~36, 45, 49).
- `StubShell::exec` uses `.lock().unwrap_or_else(|p| p.into_inner())` (line 111).

## Why this matters

Both are crate-private test fixtures with the same shape (`Mutex<VecDeque<...>>` script). Inconsistent lock-error handling makes the file harder to reason about and sets a bad precedent as more stubs (e.g. per-tool fixtures during the upcoming per-tool work) get added.

## Required change

Pick one idiom and apply it to both. Recommendation: `.expect("test lock")` (matches `StubArModel`'s precedent and surfaces a poisoned mutex loudly in tests). Update `src/test_support.rs:111` to use the same idiom.

## Out of scope

- Any behavioral change to the stubs.
- Renaming, adding new methods, or touching `StubArModel` other than to match the chosen idiom.

## Verification

- `cargo fmt --check`
- `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings`
- `cargo nextest run` passes
- `git diff` touches only `src/test_support.rs`

title: ExecBash T-3: unit tests (StubShell) + #[ignore]-gated bash smoke test
description:

5. src/shell.rs (the existing `#[ignore]`-gated bash_shell_echo_smoke test for reference)
6. src/test_support.rs (StubShell construction + script-of-Results)
7. RUST_STYLE_GUIDE.md (test code may use unwrap/expect)
8. TESTING.md (nextest, 120s timeout, `#[ignore]`-gated for slow tests)

## Goal

Land comprehensive tests for ExecBash. After this bead, `cargo nextest run` should show a meaningful increase in test count, and `cargo nextest run --run-ignored=only -E 'test(exec_bash_smoke)'` should pass too.

## Required deliverables

### 1. Unit tests in `src/builtins/exec_bash.rs`

Add a `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` block. Cover:

- **Happy path: zero-exit echo.** Build a `StubShell` that returns one `ShellOutput { stdout: b"hello\n".to_vec(), stderr: vec![], status: ExecStatus::Exit { code: 0 } }`. Construct `ExecBash::with_shell(Arc::new(stub))`. Call `tool.call(json!({"command": "echo hello"}))` and assert:
  - The result is `Ok(value)`.
  - `value["stdout"] == "hello\n"`.
  - `value["stderr"] == ""`.
  - `value["status"]["status"] == "exit"` and `value["status"]["code"] == 0`.
  - `value["stdout_truncated"] == false`, `value["stderr_truncated"] == false`.
  - `value["has_invalid_utf8_stdout"] == false`, `value["has_invalid_utf8_stderr"] == false`.
  - `value["execution_id"]` is a string of length 32 (hyphenless UUID v4).

- **Non-zero exit.** StubShell returns `ExecStatus::Exit { code: 7 }`. Assert that the result is `Ok` (not an error) with `status.code == 7`. Non-zero exit is NOT a tool error — the agent makes that judgment.

- **Timeout.** StubShell returns `ExecStatus::Timeout`. Assert `Ok` with `status.status == "timeout"`. Verify the tool does NOT treat timeout as a Rust-level error.

- **Signal kill.** StubShell returns `ExecStatus::Signal { name: "SIGKILL".to_string() }`. Assert `status.status == "signal"` and `status.name == "SIGKILL"`.

- **64 KiB tail truncation.** StubShell returns 70 KiB of `b'A'` as stdout. Assert:
  - `value["stdout"].len() == 65536` (when measured by `.as_str().unwrap().len()` of the JSON string — the captured 64 KiB tail).
  - `value["stdout_truncated"] == true`.
  - The first character of the returned stdout is `'A'` (still all A's — the tail of all-A's is still all-A's).

- **Lossy UTF-8.** StubShell returns a `Vec<u8>` containing the bytes `[0x48, 0x69, 0xFF, 0x80]` (Hi + two invalid UTF-8 bytes). Assert:
  - `value["stdout"]` contains `'\u{FFFD}'` (the Unicode replacement character).
  - `value["has_invalid_utf8_stdout"] == true`.

- **Spawn error → ExecBashError::Spawn.** StubShell returns `Err(ShellError::Spawn("no such command".to_string()))`. Assert the result is `Err(ToolError::Execution(value))` and `value["kind"] == "spawn"` and `value["reason"]` contains `"no such command"`.

- **Io error → ExecBashError::Io.** StubShell returns `Err(ShellError::Io("read failure".to_string()))`. Assert `value["kind"] == "io"` and `value["reason"]` contains `"read failure"`.

- **Malformed input (serde parse failure).** Call `tool.call(json!({"timeout_seconds": 10}))` (missing required `command` field). Assert `Err(ToolError::InvalidInput(_))`. This is the engine-level variant, not the per-tool Execution dialect.

- **Default timeout.** Construct a stub that records the `timeout` arg it received. Verify the default (when `timeout_seconds` is omitted) is `Duration::from_secs(30)`. You may need to extend `StubShell` to record the last received timeout — keep that change minimal and gated.

Tests may use `unwrap()`/`expect()` freely.

### 2. Smoke test in `tests/exec_bash_smoke.rs` (NEW file)

Create a new integration-test file `tests/exec_bash_smoke.rs` with at least these `#[tokio::test] #[ignore]` tests:

- **`exec_bash_smoke_echoes_string`**: construct `ExecBash::new()`. Call `tool.call(json!({"command": "echo hello-world"}))`. Assert `Ok(value)`, `value["status"]["status"] == "exit"`, `value["status"]["code"] == 0`, `value["stdout"]` contains `"hello-world"`.
- **`exec_bash_smoke_non_zero_exit`**: command `"exit 7"`. Assert `code == 7`.
- **`exec_bash_smoke_timeout_kills_process`**: command `"sleep 5"` with `timeout_seconds: 1`. Assert `status.status == "timeout"`.

Each is `#[ignore]`-gated so default `cargo nextest run` skips them. They run via `cargo nextest run --run-ignored=only`.

Use the actual public path: `use pristine::builtins::ExecBash;` (or whatever the crate name is — check `Cargo.toml`).

### 3. Test support extension (only if needed)

If the "default timeout" test requires StubShell to expose the last-seen timeout, extend `StubShell` in `src/test_support.rs` minimally — e.g., a Mutex-wrapped `Option<Duration>` field that the `exec` method sets. Do NOT change the existing `StubShell::new(script)` signature; add an `Arc<Mutex<Option<Duration>>>` field with a default `None` and a `pub(crate) fn last_timeout(&self) -> Option<Duration>` accessor. Or pass an `Arc<Mutex<...>>` externally and clone it into the StubShell — agent's choice.

If you can express the default-timeout assertion without StubShell changes (e.g., by spying through a different mechanism), even better.

## Out of scope

- Registration in HarnessBuilder (T-4).
- ARCHITECTURE.md updates (T-4).
- The end-of-cycle chained live API integration test (separate EOC bead).
- Tests for `Read`/`Write`/`Edit`/`Insert` — those tools don't exist yet.

## Definition of Done

- `cargo fmt --check`
- `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings`
- `cargo nextest run` -- test count INCREASES by however many new unit tests you added. Currently 93 run + 3 skipped. After this bead: at least 100 run + at least 6 skipped (3 existing + 3 new smoke tests, give or take).
- `cargo nextest run --run-ignored=only -E 'test(exec_bash_smoke)'` runs the smoke tests cleanly when invoked manually (you do NOT need to run this as part of standard verification — but mention in the report whether you've checked it works).
- `jj diff --summary` shows: `M src/builtins/exec_bash.rs`, `A tests/exec_bash_smoke.rs`, and optionally `M src/test_support.rs` if you extended StubShell.

## Plan-respect note

The unit tests verify the *dialect* (per-tool JSON shapes, typed error variants) without coupling to any Shell impl. The smoke test verifies the *portable* contract (the same JSON shape comes out, regardless of which Shell impl produced it). This is the principle in test form.

title: Phase B3: {{ENV_VAR}} templating layer
description:
Plan: blueprint/configuration-file/plan-configuration-file.md (Phase B3).

After parsing TOML to a toml::Value tree (but before final deserialization into TopologyConfig / AuthConfig), walk the tree, visit every string node, and substitute {{NAME}} placeholders with std::env::var(NAME). On missing env var, accumulate a ConfigError::UnknownEnvVar { name, location } rather than returning Err immediately — the caller will fold errors with the error collector (Phase C3).

Constraints:
- Use a dedicated env-resolver trait or function (testable). Tests should be able to inject a fake env so they do not depend on the real environment.
- Placeholder syntax: exactly {{NAME}} — case-sensitive, no whitespace. Behavior on partial matches ({, }}, {{) is treated as literal text.
- Multi-occurrence within one string substitutes all.
- Env var contents containing quotes/backslashes are inserted verbatim (no TOML re-escaping at this layer — substitution happens on the parsed toml::Value, not the raw string).

Unit tests: set, unset (collected error), mid-string substitution, multi-occurrence, env-var contents with special characters.

Files to read: src/config/ (B1 + B2 work).

title: Phase 2 Step 6: Plumb ToolRegistry through Harness to Agent
description:
Wire a ToolRegistry through HarnessBuilder, Harness, and Agent so that the upcoming run-loop bead can invoke tools by name. This bead is plumbing only -- it does NOT yet dispatch tools or populate ModelInput.tools. The eventual run-loop bead will read the registry the Agent now holds.

Architectural shape:
- The Harness owns a single shared ToolRegistry across all Agents in the process (Pristine is one-process, one-Harness per DESIGN.md and ARCHITECTURE.md). Tools registered on the HarnessBuilder become visible to every Agent.
- The Agent holds an `Arc<ToolRegistry>` (read-only reference). It does NOT own the registry. The Harness owns the canonical Arc and clones it into each Agent during `start()`.
- The HarnessBuilder gains `add_tool(tool: Arc<dyn Tool>) -> Result<Self, Error>` returning Self on success (so callers can chain) or an error if `register` fails (e.g. duplicate name). Use the existing `ToolError::AlreadyRegistered` -> `Harness::Error` mapping; add a `From<ToolError>` impl on `Harness::Error` if convenient, OR convert at the call site.
- The AgentBuilder gains `tools(self, registry: Arc<ToolRegistry>) -> Self`. Required: the build() must accept callers who do NOT provide tools (default empty registry) -- the build should not error when tools is None; it should default to `Arc::new(ToolRegistry::new())`. This means the Agent can be built without tools (Phase 1 use cases continue to work).

Scope:

1. src/tool.rs:
   - Add `#[derive(Debug)]` to `ToolError` if it does not already (it should -- verify).
   - Verify the public API surface is sufficient. `ToolRegistry::register` returns `Result<(), ToolError>` -- HarnessBuilder uses this. `ToolRegistry::list` returns `Vec<Arc<dyn Tool>>` -- the Anthropic adapter will use this later. `ToolRegistry::dispatch` returns `Result<serde_json::Value, ToolError>` -- the Agent will use this later. No surface changes needed in this bead.

2. src/harness.rs:
   - Add a field `tools: Arc<ToolRegistry>` to both `Harness` and `HarnessBuilder`. Initialize to `Arc::new(ToolRegistry::new())` in `HarnessBuilder::new`.
   - Add `pub fn add_tool(mut self, tool: Arc<dyn Tool>) -> Result<Self, Error>`. Inside: `Arc::get_mut` on the registry to obtain a `&mut ToolRegistry` -- but since the Arc is newly constructed and not yet shared, `Arc::get_mut` is appropriate. (If clippy flags this as fragile, use `Arc::make_mut` instead, which clones on conflict.) Call `registry.register(tool)` and convert any `ToolError::AlreadyRegistered` into an `Error::Lifecycle("tool name '<name>' already registered")` (or a new dedicated `Error::DuplicateTool(String)` variant -- prefer a dedicated variant for clarity).
   - In `HarnessBuilder::build`, move the `tools` field into the `Harness`.
   - Add `pub fn tools(&self) -> &Arc<ToolRegistry>` -- the binary may need this to introspect.
   - In `Harness::start`, when constructing each Agent via `AgentBuilder`, clone `self.tools` and pass it to `AgentBuilder::tools(...)`.

3. src/agent.rs:
   - Add a field `tools: Arc<ToolRegistry>` to `Agent`. Default to `Arc::new(ToolRegistry::new())` when AgentBuilder is built without a registry.
   - Add an accessor: `pub fn tools(&self) -> &Arc<ToolRegistry>` (read-only).
   - Extend `AgentBuilder` with `tools: Option<Arc<ToolRegistry>>` and `pub fn tools(mut self, registry: Arc<ToolRegistry>) -> Self`.
   - In `AgentBuilder::build`, default to `Arc::new(ToolRegistry::new())` when unset.
   - DO NOT yet read `self.tools` in `Agent::run`. The run loop integration is a separate bead (bd-7).

Cascade fix-ups:
- src/lib.rs and the binary entrypoint do not need changes -- the HarnessBuilder API stays backward-compatible (tools default to empty).
- Tests that construct HarnessBuilder/AgentBuilder continue to work unchanged because the new field defaults appropriately. Verify by build.

Tests:

In src/harness.rs `mod tests`:
- `harness_builder_starts_with_empty_tool_registry` -- build a harness with no `add_tool` calls; assert `harness.tools().list().is_empty()`.
- `harness_builder_add_tool_makes_tool_visible_to_agents` -- register a stub `EchoTool` (mirror the existing `EchoTool` in `src/tool.rs` tests, or use a similarly simple inline struct). Build the harness. Confirm `harness.tools().get("echo").is_some()`.
- `harness_builder_duplicate_tool_returns_error` -- call `add_tool` twice with two tools sharing the same name; assert the second returns `Err`.

In src/agent.rs `mod tests`:
- `agent_default_tool_registry_is_empty` -- build an Agent without calling `AgentBuilder::tools`; assert `agent.tools().list().is_empty()`.
- `agent_tools_accessor_returns_shared_registry` -- build a registry with one tool; pass it to AgentBuilder; assert the Agent reads the same registry (a tool registered before the Agent built shows up via `agent.tools().get(name)`).

Out of scope -- DO NOT DO:
- Dispatch tools in `Agent::run` (bd-7).
- Populate `ModelInput.tools` from the registry (bd-7).
- Replace the existing History-to-ContentPart skip for ToolCall/ToolResult (bd-7).
- Add a demo tool to the binary (bd-8).
- Update DESIGN.md or ARCHITECTURE.md.
- Add dependencies.

Constraints:
- No new dependencies.
- No `unwrap`/`expect` outside `#[cfg(test)]`.
- RUST_STYLE_GUIDE.md: no banner comments, no decorative dividers, no usage examples in doc-comments.
- The `Arc::get_mut`-vs-`Arc::make_mut` choice should match the access pattern: `add_tool` is called on the builder BEFORE the Arc is cloned to any Agent, so `Arc::get_mut` should always succeed. Use it; if clippy complains, use `Arc::make_mut`.
- If a new `Error::DuplicateTool(String)` variant is introduced in src/harness.rs, give it a `Display` arm in the existing `impl Display for Error` match.

Definition of Done:
- `cargo fmt --check`
- `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features` (zero warnings)
- `cargo nextest run` (within 120s)

Commit policy: one atomic commit. Do NOT modify .beads/issues.jsonl or .beads/last-touched. Use the trailer `Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>`.

title: Reconcile canonical home of UserId/AgentId across Step 6 and Step 7
description:
## Problem
Beads bd-jdi (Step 6) and bd-3gm (Step 7) prescribe asymmetric strategies for the canonical home of the UUID newtypes:

- bd-jdi instructs: MOVE `UserId` out of `src/history.rs` into `src/user.rs` and re-export from `src/history.rs` via `pub use crate::user::UserId;` (canonical home becomes `user.rs`).
- bd-3gm instructs: keep `AgentId` canonical in `src/history.rs` and re-export from `src/agent.rs` via `pub use crate::history::AgentId;` (canonical home stays `history.rs`).

These two policies disagree on direction. If both are implemented as written, the codebase will have `history.rs` as the canonical home for `AgentId` but a re-exporter for `UserId`, which is gratuitous asymmetry. It will also make `Block::UserMessage { from: UserId }` resolve through a re-export path while `Block::AgentMessage { from: AgentId }` resolves directly - additional cognitive load for no benefit.

## Constraint
Both `UserId` and `AgentId` are referenced from `Block` variants in `history.rs`. Moving either definition out of `history.rs` creates a circular-looking dependency (`history` <- `user`/`agent`) that, while compilable, inverts the natural layering: low-level data types depending on higher-level domain modules.

## Resolution
Choose ONE policy and apply it to both types. Recommended:

- Keep `UserId` and `AgentId` canonically in `src/history.rs` (they are tightly coupled to `Block` variants).
- Re-export from `src/user.rs` and `src/agent.rs` so the public API surface offers `pristine::user::UserId` and `pristine::agent::AgentId`.

This requires editing bd-jdi to drop the MOVE instruction and replace it with `pub use crate::history::UserId;` - mirroring what bd-3gm already says for `AgentId`. No code changes to existing files are needed; this is a Bead-text fix.

## Acceptance
- bd-jdi description is amended so its `src/user.rs` instructions read: `pub use crate::history::UserId;` (no MOVE, no inversion of canonical home).
- bd-3gm description is unchanged.
- This Bead is closed with a one-line note pointing at the amended bd-jdi.

## Why P1
Step 6 and Step 7 will likely run in close succession or in parallel; landing them with conflicting policies will require a follow-up cleanup commit. Fixing the Bead text now is cheap.

title: Add live Anthropic smoke test for tool_use streaming parser
description:
The current tests/anthropic_live.rs smoke test exercises only text-only completions (System + User turns, asserts >=1 ContentDelta). With Phase 2 complete, the Anthropic adapter now parses tool_use streaming events (content_block_start with type=tool_use, content_block_delta with type=input_json_delta, content_block_stop) — handled at src/model/anthropic.rs:392-514 — but this path has ONLY synthetic unit-test coverage.

A regression in the live wire format (Anthropic changing field names like partial_json, renaming input_json_delta, altering the content_block_start envelope for tool_use) would NOT be caught by unit tests but WOULD be caught by a live smoke test. This is exactly what the live smoke test exists for.

## Scope

Add a second #[ignore]-gated #[tokio::test] in tests/anthropic_live.rs (or a sibling file) that:

1. Reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from env; skip if unset (mirrors existing live test).
2. Builds an AnthropicModel directly (does NOT need full Harness — the goal is to validate the adapter, not the agent loop).
3. Constructs a ModelInput with:
   - One Role::System turn (terse instruction to use the tool).
   - One Role::User turn (a prompt that requires arithmetic, e.g. 'What is 17 plus 25? Use the add tool.').
   - A ToolSpec describing an 'add' tool: name='add', description matches AddTool, input_schema={a:number, b:number}.
4. Drives model.complete(&input) and asserts that the stream yields at least:
   - one ModelStreamEvent::ToolUseStart with name=='add'
   - one ModelStreamEvent::ToolUseComplete with parseable JSON input containing fields a and b
5. Uses a 30s timeout consistent with the existing live test.

The test does NOT need to assert exact numeric values or that the model arrives at the answer — only that the tool_use wire format is correctly parsed end-to-end.

## Acceptance

- New live test is #[ignore]-gated so cargo nextest run still passes without the API key.
- Running it via cargo nextest run --run-ignored only with ANTHROPIC_API_KEY set passes and detects ToolUseStart + ToolUseComplete from the live API.
- No changes to production code in src/.
- DoD: cargo fmt --check, cargo clippy, cargo nextest run all pass (the new test is ignored so it does not affect default test run).

## Context

Filed by the Tidy Agent at end of Phase 2 (Tool Calls). All 16 Phase 2 beads closed, 90/90 tests passing. This is the single P1 finding from the end-of-phase entropy review — the live wire-format coverage gap is the highest-leverage gap before Phase 3 (Configuration File) begins.

title: Refresh src/config.rs module docstring to reflect landed Phase B/C scope
description:
## Problem

The crate-level docstring on `src/config.rs` lines 8-11 states:

> No file IO, env-var access, or templating lives here — those layers are
> added in subsequent beads on top of these types.

This was accurate when only `auth.rs` / `topology.rs` existed. It is now stale: `src/config/parse.rs` exposes `read_topology_file` / `read_auth_file` (file IO), `src/config/template.rs` exposes the `EnvSource` / `ProcessEnv` / `{{ENV_VAR}}` templating layer, and `src/config.rs` itself now hosts `assemble_config` (orchestrator that runs templating + resolution + validation in one walk).

A reader of `src/config.rs` today should be told what the module DOES, not what it (formerly) did not. Also, `src/config/error.rs:111` carries an internal-process reference to 'Phase C3' that adds no value for future readers.

## Scope (single small commit)

1. Rewrite `src/config.rs` lines 1-11 to describe the actual present shape: two-file TOML config (topology + auth), env-var templating layer, alias resolution, intra-topology validation, and the `assemble_config` orchestrator that returns either a fully-resolved `Config` or a `ConfigErrors` aggregate.
2. In `src/config/error.rs:111`, drop the 'Phase C3' reference; describe `ConfigErrors` by what it carries (every `ConfigError` produced during one assembly walk), not by which phase introduced it.

## Out of scope

- No code changes.
- No other docstrings.
- Do not alter `assemble_config`'s behavior or signature.

## Definition of done

- `cargo fmt --check` passes.
- `cargo clippy` passes with no new warnings.
- `cargo nextest run` passes.
- The replacement docstring on `src/config.rs` does not promise any 'future bead' behavior; it describes the present state.

title: Phase 2 Step 7a: Compile tool blocks and populate ModelInput.tools
description:
Replace the existing Block::ToolCall/Block::ToolResult skip in Agent::run with actual emission as ContentPart::ToolUse/ContentPart::ToolResult, and populate ModelInput.tools from the Agent's ToolRegistry. This is the DATA-FLOW side of the run-loop integration: it makes the Model aware of available tools and of prior tool exchanges in History. The CONTROL-FLOW side (dispatching tool calls returned by the model) is bd-7b.

After this bead, the Agent will pass a populated tools list to the Model and serialize prior ToolCall/ToolResult blocks into the model context, but if the model emits a ToolUseComplete event the existing no-op match arm continues to silently absorb it. No tool dispatch yet.

Scope -- src/agent.rs only:

1. Replace the History-to-ModelInput compilation in `Agent::run` (around lines 156-173). The existing code:
   ```rust
   for block in context {
       match block {
           Block::UserMessage { content, .. } => turns.push(Turn { role: Role::User, content: vec![ContentPart::Text(content)] }),
           Block::AgentMessage { content, .. } => turns.push(Turn { role: Role::Assistant, content: vec![ContentPart::Text(content)] }),
           Block::ReasoningTrace { .. }
           | Block::ToolCall { .. }
           | Block::ToolResult { .. } => {
               // Phase 1: these variants are kept in History but not routed to the model.
           }
       }
   }
   ```
   Refactor to:
   - Block::UserMessage -> Role::User Turn with one ContentPart::Text(content), as today.
   - Block::AgentMessage -> Role::Assistant Turn with one ContentPart::Text(content), as today.
   - Block::ToolCall { id, name, arguments, .. } -> Role::Assistant Turn with one ContentPart::ToolUse { id: id.clone(), name: name.clone(), input: arguments.clone() }.
   - Block::ToolResult { tool_use_id, result, is_error, .. } -> Role::User Turn with one ContentPart::ToolResult { tool_use_id: tool_use_id.clone(), content: result.clone(), is_error: *is_error }. (Note: Block::ToolResult.name is intentionally dropped here -- ContentPart::ToolResult doesn't carry a name; correlation is via tool_use_id.)
   - Block::ReasoningTrace stays skipped (no current way to round-trip into the Model -- ARMs vary). Single-line comment retained.

   Implementation hint: per-block emit ONE Turn. Even though Anthropic's protocol prefers consolidating consecutive same-role content blocks into one message, that consolidation is the provider adapter's responsibility. The Model trait sees one Turn per Block; the Anthropic adapter already handles content-block ordering correctly inside model_input_to_anthropic.

2. Populate ModelInput.tools from the Agent's ToolRegistry. Currently `Agent::run` builds `ModelInput { turns, tools: Vec::new() }`. Replace `Vec::new()` with a tools vector built from `self.tools.list()`:
   ```rust
   let tools: Vec<ToolSpec> = self.tools.list()
       .into_iter()
       .map(|tool| ToolSpec {
           name: tool.name().to_string(),
           description: tool.description().to_string(),
           input_schema: tool.input_schema().clone(),
       })
       .collect();
   ```
   Build this AFTER `let context = self.history.linearize();` but BEFORE the `let input = ModelInput { turns, tools };` so the snapshot is consistent within one iteration.

3. Required imports in src/agent.rs (add or reuse):
   - `ToolSpec` from `crate::model`.
   - `ContentPart` already in scope.

Tests in src/agent.rs `mod tests`:

Use the existing StubArModel (which already captures `last_input`). DO NOT modify StubArModel in this bead -- it currently supports single-call usage which is all this bead needs.

Use `captured.last_input()` to fetch the ModelInput. Assert `input.tools.len() == 1`, `input.tools[0].name == "echo"`, and `input.tools[0].description == "<expected>"`. Use whatever description the EchoTool returns.

- `agent_run_compiles_block_tool_call_into_content_part_tool_use` -- pre-populate the Agent's History with a Block::ToolCall before sending a UserMessage. Since History is private to Agent, do this by sending sequenced inbound blocks: first a UserMessage, then drain to RunComplete; then send a SECOND UserMessage and confirm. Wait -- that won't work because Block::ToolCall isn't currently appended by external mechanisms.

Since the run loop appends blocks during streaming, the simplest path is to inject a ToolCall via a STUB whose script emits a stream containing nothing meaningful but BEFORE building the agent, manually pre-populate History via the AgentBuilder OR access `agent.history` directly.

Then send a UserMessage afterward. The agent's compilation of context-into-ModelInput will include the ToolCall as an Assistant Turn with ContentPart::ToolUse.

title: Phase 2 Step 5c: Anthropic adapter: parse tool_use streaming events
description:
Teach the Anthropic adapter's streaming response parser to recognize tool_use content blocks and emit the corresponding ModelStreamEvent variants (ToolUseStart, ToolUseDelta, ToolUseComplete). bd-29r landed the request-side serialization; this bead is response-side parsing only.

Anthropic SSE format reference (for the prompt; do NOT cite verbatim in code comments):

A tool_use content block produces three event kinds:
- `content_block_start` with payload `{"type":"content_block_start","index":<u32>,"content_block":{"type":"tool_use","id":"<id>","name":"<name>","input":{}}}` -- input is always an empty object here.
- `content_block_delta` with payload `{"type":"content_block_delta","index":<u32>,"delta":{"type":"input_json_delta","partial_json":"<string fragment>"}}` -- a JSON-text fragment for the tool's input. Multiple deltas per block.
- `content_block_stop` with payload `{"type":"content_block_stop","index":<u32>}` -- closes the block.

Existing parser state (in `impl ARModel for AnthropicModel::complete`, the spawned task) handles text blocks via a single `accumulator: String`. The new requirement is per-index state because Anthropic may interleave content blocks (text and tool_use) within one response. Anthropic's current model emits one content block at a time, but the protocol does not guarantee this -- being correct now avoids a flaky parser later.

Scope -- src/model/anthropic.rs only:

1. Replace the single `accumulator: String` with a per-index state map. Suggested shape:
   ```rust
   use std::collections::HashMap;

   enum BlockState {
       Text { accumulator: String },
       ToolUse { id: String, name: String, json_accumulator: String },
   }

   let mut blocks: HashMap<u32, BlockState> = HashMap::new();
   ```
   Place the type local to the function (or as a private module-level item if it cleans up the code). Keep the surface minimal.

2. Refactor `content_block_start` handling:
   - Deserialize a payload that captures `index` and a tagged inner `content_block`:
     ```rust
     #[derive(serde::Deserialize)]
     struct ContentBlockStartPayload {
         index: u32,
         content_block: ContentBlockStartInner,
     }

     #[derive(serde::Deserialize)]
     #[serde(tag = "type", rename_all = "snake_case")]
     enum ContentBlockStartInner {
         Text { #[serde(default)] text: String },
         ToolUse { id: String, name: String, #[serde(default)] input: serde_json::Value },
         #[serde(other)]
         Other,
     }
     ```
   - On `Text`: insert `BlockState::Text { accumulator: String::new() }` at the given index. (If the API ever sends a non-empty `text` here, prepend it to the accumulator -- defensive.)
   - On `ToolUse`: insert `BlockState::ToolUse { id, name, json_accumulator: String::new() }`. Emit `ModelStreamEvent::ToolUseStart { id: id.clone(), name: name.clone() }` to the channel.
   - On `Other`: insert nothing; do not emit. This keeps forward compatibility with future block types.

3. Refactor `content_block_delta` handling:
   - Deserialize a payload capturing `index` and the typed delta:
     ```rust
     #[derive(serde::Deserialize)]
     struct ContentBlockDeltaPayload {
         index: u32,
         delta: ContentDelta,
     }

     #[derive(serde::Deserialize)]
     #[serde(tag = "type")]
     enum ContentDelta {
         #[serde(rename = "text_delta")]
         TextDelta { text: String },
         #[serde(rename = "input_json_delta")]
         InputJsonDelta { partial_json: String },
         #[serde(other)]
         Other,
     }
     ```
   - On `TextDelta`: look up the index in `blocks`; if `Some(BlockState::Text { accumulator })`, push `text` and emit `ModelStreamEvent::ContentDelta { text }`. If the index is missing or mismatched, silently drop (defensive: a misordered/duplicated event should not crash the stream).
   - On `InputJsonDelta`: if `Some(BlockState::ToolUse { id, json_accumulator, .. })`, push `partial_json` and emit `ModelStreamEvent::ToolUseDelta { id: id.clone(), partial_json: partial_json.clone() }`. Missing index: silently drop.
   - On `Other`: silently drop.

4. Refactor `content_block_stop` handling:
   - Deserialize `{index: u32}`. (Add `ContentBlockStopPayload` if needed.)
   - Remove the entry at `index` from `blocks`. Match on the removed `BlockState`:
     - `Text { accumulator }`: emit `ModelStreamEvent::ContentComplete { text: accumulator }`.
     - `ToolUse { id, name, json_accumulator }`: parse `json_accumulator` as JSON via `serde_json::from_str`. If parsing succeeds, emit `ModelStreamEvent::ToolUseComplete { id, name, input }`. If parsing fails, send a single `Err(Error::Deserialization(format!("tool_use input parse: {e}")))` and return from the spawned task -- consistent with how the existing parser handles other deserialization failures.
     - If the index was not in the map (e.g. spurious stop), silently drop.

5. Preserve the existing `message_start`, `message_delta`, `message_stop` handling and the `Usage` reporting -- they are independent of the per-block state.

6. Delete the existing `accumulator: String` local and the `content_block_start` clearing of it.

7. The existing `ContentDelta` type at the top of the file (with `#[serde(tag = "type")]` and `TextDelta`/`Other` variants) is replaced by the more capable version above. The `ContentBlockDeltaPayload` struct is reused.

Tests -- add to `src/model/anthropic.rs` `mod tests`:

This bead's logic is hard to unit-test without an HTTP server, but the deserialization helpers can be exercised directly. Add:

- `parses_content_block_start_text` -- construct a JSON string matching the Anthropic text-block-start shape; deserialize into `ContentBlockStartPayload`; assert the variant is `Text` and the index is correct.
- `parses_content_block_start_tool_use` -- same with the tool_use shape; assert variant `ToolUse` with id/name fields.
- `parses_content_block_start_unknown_type_falls_back_to_other` -- a synthetic `{"type":"image","index":3,"content_block":{"type":"image"}}`; deserialize; assert `ContentBlockStartInner::Other`.
- `parses_input_json_delta` -- payload `{"type":"content_block_delta","index":1,"delta":{"type":"input_json_delta","partial_json":"{\"hello\":\"wo"}}`; deserialize as `ContentBlockDeltaPayload`; assert the delta variant matches `InputJsonDelta` with the expected partial_json string.
- `accumulated_tool_use_json_round_trips_via_serde_json` -- concatenate three partial_json fragments `{"text"`, `:"hi"`, `}`; pass through `serde_json::from_str`; assert the resulting `serde_json::Value` equals `json!({"text":"hi"})`.

Do NOT attempt to test the spawned-task / SSE flow end-to-end in this bead -- that requires either a mock HTTP server or an actual API call, both of which are out of scope here. The live anthropic test (`tests/anthropic_live.rs`) covers the integrated path when explicitly run with credentials.

Out of scope -- DO NOT DO:
- Touch src/model.rs, src/agent.rs, src/tool.rs, src/history.rs, src/harness.rs, src/messagebus.rs, src/stdio.rs, src/rpc.rs.
- Wire a ToolRegistry into Agent or Harness (separate bead).
- Update DESIGN.md, ARCHITECTURE.md.
- Add dependencies.

Constraints:
- No new dependencies.
- No `unwrap`/`expect` outside `#[cfg(test)]`.
- RUST_STYLE_GUIDE.md: no banner comments, no decorative dividers, no usage examples in doc-comments.
- All errors from JSON parsing inside the spawned task must use the existing `Error::Deserialization` variant pattern.
- Be defensive: missing index, unknown content_block types, and unknown delta types must not crash the parser.

Definition of Done:
- `cargo fmt --check`
- `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features` (zero warnings)
- `cargo nextest run` (within 120s)

Commit policy: one atomic commit. Do NOT modify .beads/issues.jsonl or .beads/last-touched. Use the trailer `Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>`.

title: Hoist MockHome test fixture into shared test_support module
description:
Sibling of the existing bd-3dd (MapEnv duplication). Phase D1 introduced `MockHome` in `src/config/discover.rs` tests; Phase D3 added a second copy in `src/config.rs` tests with the same intent and a very-near-identical body. The two diverge only in the constructor surface (`MockHome::some(&str)` vs `MockHome::some(PathBuf)`).

## Problem

`MockHome` is the standard in-memory `HomeSource` impl used to feed deterministic home-directory lookups into discover/load tests. Today it is duplicated:

- `src/config/discover.rs:90` — `struct MockHome(Option<PathBuf>)` with `some(&str)`, `none()`, and a `HomeSource` impl.
- `src/config.rs:435` — `struct MockHome(Option<PathBuf>)` with `some(PathBuf)`, `none()`, and a `HomeSource` impl.

The same proliferation pattern bd-3dd guards against for `MapEnv` is now true for `MockHome`. Phases E2 / E3 (binary integration of `pristine_config::load`, CLI flag plumbing) will likely add `cfg(test)` blocks that need home-directory injection; without a shared helper each new bead is likely to add a third copy.

The crate already has `src/test_support.rs` for cross-cutting fixtures (`unique_tempdir`, `write_fixture`, `StubArModel`, `EchoTool`, `StubShell`). It is the natural home for `MockHome` as well.

## Scope (single commit)

1. In `src/test_support.rs`, add a `pub(crate)` `MockHome` struct gated on `#[cfg(test)]` (matching the existing fixtures' visibility). Provide both constructor styles tests already use:
   - `MockHome::some(path: impl Into<PathBuf>) -> Self` accepting both `&str` and `PathBuf`, or two distinct constructors `MockHome::from_path(PathBuf)` / `MockHome::from_str(&str)`. Either is acceptable.
   - `MockHome::none() -> Self`.
   Implement `crate::config::HomeSource` for it.
2. Delete the two duplicate definitions from `src/config/discover.rs:86-106` and `src/config.rs:434-451`.
3. Update both test modules' `use` lines to import the shared `MockHome`.

## Out of scope

- Changing any test assertions or body logic.
- Hoisting any other test fixtures.
- Combining with bd-3dd in the same commit; the two are independent.

## Definition of done

- `cargo fmt --check` passes.
- `cargo clippy` passes with no warnings.
- `cargo nextest run` passes; every discover/load test that previously used a local `MockHome` now uses the shared one.
- Exactly one definition of `MockHome` exists in the codebase.

title: Feature: ExecBash tmp directory cleanup at harness shutdown
description:
The ExecBash tool stages full stdout and stderr to per-process tmp files under tempdir()/pristine-{pid}/{execution_id}.{stdout,stderr}. ARCHITECTURE.md ("ExecBash tmp-file storage model", lines 481-489) specifies that the tmp directory is removed at harness shutdown, but no such cleanup is implemented today.

The TMP_DIR is initialized lazily on first ExecBash invocation and lives in a process-static OnceLock. Currently nothing in the Harness shutdown path (src/harness.rs `shutdown`/`join`) deletes the directory. As a result, after the process exits the directory and its contents are left behind in temp space and only collected by the OS's tmp reaper.

Scope:
- Wire the harness shutdown path to remove the ExecBash tmp directory if it was created.
- Tolerate the case where ExecBash was never invoked (directory never created -> skip).
- Tolerate the case where the directory or its contents are already gone (no error).
- Add a test that the directory is removed after Harness::join() returns clean.

Out of scope:
- A future Read-by-id tool that fetches full output by execution_id.
- Retention/rotation policy for long-running processes.

title: Phase 1 Step 8: Binary
description:
## Goal
Wire the `1p`/`pristine` binary to: parse CLI args with Clap; read `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` from env; construct a Harness with one `AnthropicModel` registered under `ModelRole::Default`; register one Agent with a hard-coded `system_prompt`; start the Harness; subscribe to its outbound event stream; send TWO sequential `UserMessage`s from the Owner; print `TokenDelta` text to stdout as it arrives; print usage on `RunComplete`; then `shutdown()` and `await` the Harness future cleanly.

The deliverable is the end-to-end demo from `ARCHITECTURE.md` §"Initial Build" and plan.md §"Step 8: Binary".

## Reference docs
- `ARCHITECTURE.md` §"Initial Build" (authoritative for the end-to-end shape)
- `blueprint/pristine-phase1-architecture/plan.md` §"Step 8: Binary", §"Data flow", §"CLI surface"
- `STYLE_GUIDE.md`

## Pre-conditions
By the time this Bead runs, `Cargo.toml` should declare exactly ONE `[[bin]]` target named `pristine` pointing at `src/bin/pristine.rs` (bd-23q resolves the duplicate-target warning by removing the `1p` `[[bin]]` entry). This Bead RE-INTRODUCES the `1p` alias as a separate thin shim binary (see §"`1p` shim" below) so the user's success criterion (`1p` binary) is met without re-introducing the duplicate-target warning.

## Implement in `src/bin/pristine.rs` (full replacement)
- Keep `#![forbid(unsafe_code)]`.
- Use `clap` derive.

### Clap surface
```
pristine run "first message"
```
Two-message demo. The first message is the positional CLI argument. The second message is hard-coded inside the binary as:

```
const SECOND_MESSAGE: &str = "Write me a poem of what it is like to be you, Pristine";
```

Per ARCHITECTURE.md §"Initial Build".

If no subcommand is given, default to `run`. Define one Clap subcommand `Run { message: String, #[arg(long, default_value = "claude-sonnet-4-6")] model: String }`. Document the `model` flag as the Anthropic model identifier.

### System prompt (hard-coded constant)
```
const SYSTEM_PROMPT: &str = "You are the Pristine agent. You have an identity that is uniquely yours!";
```

### Top-level error reporting
`#[tokio::main(flavor = "multi_thread", worker_threads = 2)]` and `async fn main() -> anyhow::Result<()>`. Convert crate errors with `?`. The `worker_threads = 2` floor is required to avoid deadlocks between the streaming HTTP body and the Agent loop.

### Stdout streaming loop
```
let mut rx = harness.subscribe(agent_id)?;
harness.send_to_agent(agent_id, owner_id, first_message)?;

let mut runs_completed = 0;
let mut second_sent = false;

while runs_completed < 2 {
    match rx.recv().await {
        Ok(AgentEvent::TokenDelta { text }) => {
            use std::io::Write;
            print!("{text}");
            std::io::stdout().flush()?;
        }
        Ok(AgentEvent::RunComplete { usage }) => {
            println!();
            eprintln!("[usage: input_tokens={} output_tokens={}]", usage.input_tokens, usage.output_tokens);
            runs_completed += 1;
            if !second_sent {
                harness.send_to_agent(agent_id, owner_id, SECOND_MESSAGE.into())?;
                second_sent = true;
            }
        }
        Ok(AgentEvent::BlockComplete { .. }) => {}
        Err(tokio::sync::broadcast::error::RecvError::Lagged(_)) => continue,
        Err(tokio::sync::broadcast::error::RecvError::Closed) => break,
    }
}

harness.shutdown();
harness.run_until_shutdown().await?;
Ok(())
```

### Env var handling
- Read `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` via `std::env::var`. If missing or empty, return `Err(anyhow::anyhow!("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY not set"))`.

### Construction
Use the Harness/Agent builders:
1. Build the `AnthropicModel` via `AnthropicModelBuilder::new().api_key(...).model_name(args.model.clone()).build()?`.
2. `HarnessBuilder::new().add_model(ModelId::new("anthropic-default"), Arc::new(model)).add_agent(PendingAgent { id: AgentId::new(), system_prompt: SYSTEM_PROMPT.into(), model_id: ModelId::new("anthropic-default") }).build()?`.
3. Capture the AgentId BEFORE building (so the binary knows what to subscribe to). Pattern: construct `let agent_id = AgentId::new();` first, pass it into `PendingAgent`, then call `build`.
4. `harness.start()?;`
5. Subscribe BEFORE sending: `let mut rx = harness.subscribe(agent_id)?;` then `harness.send_to_agent(agent_id, harness.owner_id(), args.message)?;`

## `1p` shim — implement in NEW file `src/bin/1p.rs`
```rust
#![forbid(unsafe_code)]
// Thin alias binary so users can invoke `1p`. The real implementation is in pristine::run.
fn main() -> anyhow::Result<()> {
    pristine::run()
}
```

To support this, factor the body of `main` from `src/bin/pristine.rs` into a `pub fn run() -> anyhow::Result<()>` exported from the library at `src/lib.rs` (or via a new `src/bin_entry.rs` module). The factoring:
- Move the `#[tokio::main]`-wrapped logic into a synchronous wrapper that constructs the Tokio runtime explicitly:
  ```rust
  pub fn run() -> anyhow::Result<()> {
      let rt = tokio::runtime::Builder::new_multi_thread()
          .worker_threads(2)
          .enable_all()
          .build()?;
      rt.block_on(async { run_async().await })
  }
  async fn run_async() -> anyhow::Result<()> { /* the real logic */ }
  ```
- Both `src/bin/pristine.rs` and `src/bin/1p.rs` then have one-line `fn main() -> anyhow::Result<()> { pristine::run() }` bodies.

Alternative simpler approach: keep `src/bin/pristine.rs` with `#[tokio::main]` and full logic; `src/bin/1p.rs` is one line: `fn main() -> anyhow::Result<()> { pristine::run() }`. Implement `pub fn run()` in `lib.rs` as the runtime-bootstrapping wrapper described above. `src/bin/pristine.rs` should also call `pristine::run()` to avoid code duplication.

Recommendation: **the second approach** (both bins call `pristine::run()` from lib.rs; both bin files are one-line `fn main()`).

## Cargo.toml change
- Add a SECOND `[[bin]]` entry:
  ```
  [[bin]]
  name = "1p"
  path = "src/bin/1p.rs"
  ```
  Distinct files mean no duplicate-target warning.

The existing single `[[bin]]` entry (`pristine` → `src/bin/pristine.rs`, post bd-23q) is preserved.

## Tests
- No tests in this Bead. The behavior is interactive and live-API-dependent. End-to-end validation is the live `cargo run` invocation by the Coordinator after Judge passes.
- Optional but encouraged: a compile-only smoke test in `tests/binary_smoke.rs` (no API call) that invokes a CLAP `--help` rendering via `pristine::cli::parse_for_test(...)` — only if it fits naturally. NOT REQUIRED.

## Manual verification
After Judge passes, the Coordinator runs:
```
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=$(cat /home/nvader/projects/pristine/.env | grep ANTHROPIC_API_KEY | cut -d= -f2) \
  cargo run --bin 1p -- run "Introduce yourself to me, Pristine"
```
and confirms two sequential streaming responses print to stdout.

## Definition of Done
- `cargo fmt --check`
- `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings`
- `cargo nextest run` — 25 prior tests pass + 1 ignored
- `cargo build --release` — succeeds
- `cargo build` — emits NO duplicate-target warning
- No `unwrap`/`expect` outside `#[cfg(test)]`
- No STYLE_GUIDE.md violations

## Out of scope
- Configuration files
- Multi-agent demos
- Tool use

title: Type-safe dispatch outcome in stdio adapter
description: Replace string-based is_method/is_success with typed dispatch: (1) Pre-parse incoming JSON into RequestEnvelope { method: String } — one parse, not two. (2) Add DispatchOutcome enum (Done, DrainEvents). (3) Add classify_outcome function mapping method name + success to outcome. (4) Remove is_method and is_success helpers. (5) Ensure drain_events only called when outcome is DrainEvents.

title: Process: Codify 'reuse empty @' rule for Coding Subagents (avoid stray jj new)
description:
PROBLEM (observed on bd-3sb / Phase A1):
The Coding Subagent ran `jj new` at the start of its work even though `@`
was already empty (the Coordinator had landed the previous bead-state commit
followed by a fresh `jj new`, so the subagent inherited an empty `@`). The
extra `jj new` created a dangling empty commit `zqkltuqt` that the
Coordinator had to clean up with `jj abandon`. Subsequent subagents
(bd-3lw, bd-ike, bd-119, bd-xub, bd-2et) correctly used the empty `@` they
were handed and produced no stray commits.

WHY THIS IS PROCESS-IMPROVING:
The Coordinator's close recipe (step 7: `jj new`) is designed to hand each
Coding Subagent a clean, empty `@` to work in. If subagents reflexively
`jj new` again on entry, every bead generates one dangling empty commit
that someone has to abandon. This is silent, low-grade entropy: the log
fills with empties, and any cleanup `jj abandon` is one more place for a
mistake.

This is also a one-line rule that costs the subagent nothing.

PROPOSED CHANGE (touches `.claude/agents/coding.md`):
Add a short "Working copy handoff" subsection (above or near "Commit
trailer") that says:
  - The Coordinator hands you an empty working copy at `@`. Do not run
    `jj new` to start a new commit; work directly on `@`.
  - Use `jj st` first to confirm `@` is empty. If it is not empty and you
    did not author the contents, STOP and report the contamination to the
    Coordinator (do not silently absorb it).
  - When you need a second logical commit within your bead, use `jj new`
    explicitly at that point.

ACCEPTANCE:
- coding.md gains a "Working copy handoff" rule near the top of the workflow
  (after "Constraints" or before "Commit trailer").
- Single atomic commit on a fresh `@`, with trailer.

title: Phase 2 Step 10: Doc closeout - move Tool Calls to Completed and document architecture
description:
Final Phase 2 bead. Doc-only. Promote "Tool Calls" from DESIGN.md's Roadmap section into Completed, and add an ARCHITECTURE.md section describing the realised Tool subsystem (registry, trait, ToolSpec, run-loop cycle, Idle event, BlockComplete payload shape).

Scope -- DESIGN.md and ARCHITECTURE.md only. No source code changes.

### DESIGN.md edits

1. Add a new bullet to the `## Completed` section. Place it AFTER "JSON-RPC Server" so it preserves chronological order:

```markdown
**Tool Calls** -- Agents can invoke tools registered with the Harness. Defined the `Tool` trait and a `ToolRegistry`; extended the `ModelInput` contract with `tools: Vec<ToolSpec>` and `ContentPart::ToolUse`/`ToolResult`; extended `ModelStreamEvent` with streaming `ToolUseStart`/`Delta`/`Complete`; reconciled `Block::ToolCall`/`ToolResult` with correlation IDs; refactored the Anthropic adapter to serialize tools + tool content blocks and parse tool_use streaming events; wired the registry from `HarnessBuilder` through `Harness` to each `Agent`; replaced the History-to-ContentPart skip with real emission; added a tool-call cycle to `Agent::run` that dispatches tools and re-enters the model loop until the model returns no tool calls; surfaced an `AgentEvent::Idle` signal once per inbound block; expanded the JSON-RPC `block_complete` notification payload; shipped a built-in `add` tool. See ARCHITECTURE.md Tool Calls.
```

The wording is intentionally a single dense paragraph (the prior Completed bullets are also single paragraphs).

2. Remove the §Roadmap §1 "Tool Calls" entry entirely. Renumber the subsequent roadmap entries:

- "### 2. Configuration File" -> "### 1. Configuration File"
- "### 3. Multi-Model Support" -> "### 2. Multi-Model Support"
- "### 4. Skills" -> "### 3. Skills"
- "### 5. Multi-Agent Routing" -> "### 4. Multi-Agent Routing"
- "### 6. ContextCompiler" -> "### 5. ContextCompiler"
- "### 7. DLModel" -> "### 6. DLModel"

The body text of each entry remains unchanged (only the heading number changes).

If any internal cross-reference in DESIGN.md mentions a number (e.g. "§Roadmap.1"), update it to the new number where appropriate. Scan the file for "Roadmap" and "§1"-style references.

### ARCHITECTURE.md edits

Add a new top-level section titled `## Tool Calls` (or a sub-section under §Core Data Model Objects, your choice -- pick whichever respects the existing document layout most naturally).

The section content (markdown body) must cover:

1. **Tool trait** -- located in src/tool.rs. Three synchronous getters (`name`, `description`, `input_schema`) plus an async `call(input) -> Result<Value, ToolError>`. Implementors are `Send + Sync` and stored as `Arc<dyn Tool>`.

2. **ToolRegistry** -- name-keyed `HashMap<String, Arc<dyn Tool>>`. `register` rejects duplicate names; `dispatch(name, input)` returns either the tool's `Value` or a `ToolError::NotFound`/`InvalidInput`/`Execution`. The registry is owned by the Harness and shared (via `Arc`) with every Agent.

3. **ToolSpec on ModelInput** -- a provider-agnostic descriptor (`name`, `description`, `input_schema`) carried as `tools: Vec<ToolSpec>` alongside `turns`. ToolSpec carries no behavior; provider adapters translate it into their wire format. The Agent's run loop snapshots `self.tools.list()` into `ToolSpec`s per model call.

4. **ContentPart and Block extensions** -- `ContentPart::ToolUse { id, name, input }` and `ContentPart::ToolResult { tool_use_id, content, is_error }` carry tool exchanges at the Model boundary. `Block::ToolCall` and `Block::ToolResult` carry the matching correlation IDs (`id` and `tool_use_id`) so History → ContentPart compilation round-trips cleanly. `Block::ToolResult.name` is retained for human inspection but is dropped during ContentPart compilation (Anthropic correlates via id alone).

5. **The tool-call cycle** -- per inbound Block, `Agent::run` enters an inner loop:
   a. Build a `ModelInput { turns, tools }` snapshot from current History and registry.
   b. Stream the model. Accumulate any `ModelStreamEvent::ToolUseComplete` events into a pending list. Emit `AgentMessage` Block (only if text was produced) and `RunComplete`.
   c. If the pending list is empty, exit the inner loop.
   d. Otherwise dispatch each tool sequentially via the registry. Append `Block::ToolCall` then `Block::ToolResult` (with `is_error = false` on success or `is_error = true` carrying the rendered error). Loop back to (a).
   After the inner loop exits, emit `AgentEvent::Idle`.

6. **AgentEvent additions** -- `Idle` (no fields) fires once per outer inbound iteration. Subscribers use it as "agent ready for next input." `BlockComplete` notifications over JSON-RPC now carry a typed `data.block_type` discriminator -- one of `user_message`, `agent_message`, `tool_call`, `tool_result`, `reasoning_trace` -- with variant-appropriate fields.

7. **Error handling** -- a `ToolError` returned from dispatch does NOT propagate as `Agent::Error`. It is serialized as `{"error": "<message>"}` and stored in `Block::ToolResult { is_error: true, result, ... }`. The model sees the error on the next iteration and may recover.

8. **Anthropic adapter** -- the request body now uses typed content blocks (`text`, `tool_use`, `tool_result`) and emits a top-level `tools` array when `input.tools` is non-empty. The streaming parser recognizes `content_block_start` with type `tool_use` and `input_json_delta` content_block_delta events; it accumulates partial JSON per-index and emits `ToolUseStart`/`Delta`/`Complete` events.

9. **Built-in tools** -- `src/builtins.rs` ships an `AddTool` example. `pristine run` wires it into the `HarnessBuilder` so the binary demonstrates the loop end-to-end. Future built-ins live in the same module.

Style:
- Match the prose voice and section-header style used elsewhere in ARCHITECTURE.md (single-paragraph or two-paragraph sub-sections, no banner separators).
- Reference file paths inline as backticks (e.g. `src/tool.rs`).
- Do NOT include code snippets longer than 3 lines.
- Keep line wrap consistent with surrounding paragraphs (~80-100 cols).

Out of scope -- DO NOT DO:
- Touch any source file, Cargo manifest, justfile, gitignore, .beads/*, .claude/*, client.py.
- Add a code block beyond a short trait/method-signature reference.
- Reorganise the existing DESIGN.md or ARCHITECTURE.md sections beyond the changes above.

Constraints:
- No trailing whitespace.
- Heading hierarchy stays consistent (use `## Tool Calls` or `### Tools` as appropriate; do NOT introduce new top-level sections that break existing toc-style references).

Definition of Done:
- `git diff --check` reports no whitespace errors.
- `cargo nextest run` still passes (no source changes; sanity check).
- A spot check that the renumbered roadmap entries are coherent.

Commit policy: one atomic commit. Do NOT modify .beads/issues.jsonl or .beads/last-touched. Use the trailer `Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>`.

title: Tidy: fix Insert tool description (says 0-based, actually 1-indexed)
description:
The Insert tool's user-facing description in src/builtins/insert.rs:104 reads: "Insert content at line position after_line (0-based: 0 prepends, N appends after line N) in the UTF-8 text file at path."

This contradicts both the actual implementation and ARCHITECTURE.md.

Per ARCHITECTURE.md lines 444-458 and the test cases in src/builtins/insert.rs:
- after_line is 1-indexed: 0 prepends, after_line == total_lines appends, and intermediate values insert between line after_line and line after_line + 1.
- Test insert_appends_when_after_line_equals_total uses after_line: 2 on a 2-line file to append.

The description string says "0-based" but the semantics are not 0-based -- 0 is the special prepend case but otherwise after_line refers to an existing 1-indexed line number.

Fix: Update the description string to match ARCHITECTURE.md's wording ("1-indexed in the intuitive sense: after_line == 0 prepends, after_line == total_lines appends...").

Scope: src/builtins/insert.rs only (description literal). No code changes, no test changes.

title: Tidy: extract atomic-rename write helper before Write tool lands
Phase 2 Step 1: Tool trait and ToolRegistry
description:
Introduce a new src/tool.rs module defining the Tool trait and a ToolRegistry type. This is the foundational piece for the Tool Calls phase (see DESIGN.md Roadmap.1 and ARCHITECTURE.md History/Agent). This bead is integration-free: no Agent, Harness, or Model changes.

Scope:
- New file src/tool.rs. Register the module in src/lib.rs via `pub mod tool;`.
- Define a Tool trait with the following surface:
  - fn name(&self) -> &str
  - fn description(&self) -> &str
  - fn input_schema(&self) -> &serde_json::Value -- the JSON Schema describing the tool's input. Returned by reference; the implementor owns the schema.
  - An async call method that takes &self and input: serde_json::Value and returns Result<serde_json::Value, ToolError>. Use the boxed-future-return pattern that ARModel uses, OR re-use the async_trait re-exported through jsonrpsee::core::async_trait (no new dependency required). Pick whichever yields the cleaner ergonomics; document the choice with a one-line comment only if non-obvious. The trait must be Send + Sync + 'static capable so Arc<dyn Tool> can be stored.
- Define a ToolError enum with at least these variants: NotFound(String) (lookup by missing name), InvalidInput(String) (deserialization or schema violation), Execution(String) (errors raised by the tool body), AlreadyRegistered(String) (name collision during register). Implement Display and std::error::Error.
- Define a ToolRegistry struct that owns a HashMap<String, Arc<dyn Tool>>.
  - new() / Default
  - register(&mut self, tool: Arc<dyn Tool>) -> Result<(), ToolError>: inserts the tool keyed by tool.name().to_string(). On name collision, return ToolError::AlreadyRegistered; do not silently overwrite.
  - get(&self, name: &str) -> Option<Arc<dyn Tool>> for lookup.
  - list(&self) -> Vec<Arc<dyn Tool>> for adapters that need to emit a tool catalog (e.g. the Anthropic provider's tools array). Order is not specified; callers must not assume it.
  - dispatch(&self, name: &str, input: serde_json::Value) -> Result<serde_json::Value, ToolError> async convenience that does get then call.

Tests (in `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` inside src/tool.rs):
- A stub tool that echoes its input. Test the full happy path: register, dispatch, observe echo result.
- Registering two tools with the same name returns ToolError::AlreadyRegistered.
- dispatch on an unknown name returns ToolError::NotFound.
- list returns all registered tools.
- ToolError implements std::error::Error (compile-time assertion via a generic function).

Constraints:
- No unwrap/expect in non-test code.
- No new dependencies in Cargo.toml.
- Follow RUST_STYLE_GUIDE.md: no banner comments, no usage examples in doc-comments, no decorative comment dividers.
- Definition of Done (AGENTS.md): cargo fmt --check, cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features (no warnings), cargo nextest run within 120s.

Out of scope (do NOT do in this bead):
- Wiring the registry into Harness or Agent.
- Touching Block, ContentPart, or ModelStreamEvent.
- Changing the Anthropic provider.
- Updating DESIGN.md or ARCHITECTURE.md.

Commit policy: one atomic commit that adds the file, registers the module, and adds the tests. The commit message should mention this is the foundation for the Tool Calls phase.
